target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "id": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
  "name": "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof",
  "delivery": { "mode": "none", "channel": "last" },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 14308,
    "lastDeliveryStatus": "not-requested",
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "jobId": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
      "action": "finished",
      "status": "ok",
      "summary": "OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK",
      "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2",
      "durationMs": 14308,
      "model": "gpt-5.5",
      "provider": "codex-cli",
      "delivered": false,
      "deliveryStatus": "not-requested"
    }
  ],
  "total": 1,
  "hasMore": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real local gateway accepted the cron job, ran it through the registered `codex-cli` backend, recovered `OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK` during the repair flow, and recorded the run as `status: "ok"`. Delivery was not requested by design because the proof command used `--no-deliver`; the current code keeps the CLI mode explicit, resumes the first CLI session for repair, and does not hit the earlier native-tool fail-closed error.

What was not tested: External WhatsApp/Telegram send was not exercised; delivery was disabled to keep the local proof isolated and non-invasive.
Current-head ownership, lint, and Greptile cleanup verification after commit `d6ed463c96`:

```text
# RED before the ownership fix:
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
FAIL passes senderIsOwner=false to external-hook CLI cron runs

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
FAIL preserves external-hook non-owner scope during CLI empty-output repair
AssertionError: expected true to be false

# GREEN after the ownership fix:
$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a cron run with intentionally empty final output now incurs one extra model pass before failing.
  - Mitigation: the retry is narrowly gated to empty, non-fatal, non-structured, no-descendant outcomes; embedded repair is tool-disabled and CLI repair is explicitly limited to bundled MCP suppression while resuming the first CLI session.
- Risk: CLI repair mode could accidentally preserve bundled MCP wiring.

target.title: perf(reply): hoist direct-send fragment index
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Final reply payload processing rebuilt the directly-sent assistant fragment index inside every payload check. Multi-payload replies therefore rescanned all streamed block payloads repeatedly.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fragment map is now built once per reply-payload collection and reused by the direct-send predicate. Matching, metadata scoping, and media handling are unchanged.

## User Impact

Replies with many streamed payloads spend less CPU in duplicate suppression and direct-send preservation.

## Evidence

- Focused tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts`
- Result: 65 tests passed.
- Fresh autoreview: clean, no accepted/actionable findings.
- Rebased source commit: `914810b2bc`

target.title: feat(tools): introduce group:core for all built-in core tools
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `group:openclaw` is documented as "all built-in tools" but actually filters out filesystem and shell runtime tools, so there is no obvious `group:*` shorthand for "all built-in core tools." - Why it matters: operators writing `tools.allow: ["group:openclaw"]` expecting broad built-in access get a curated subset instead. A dedicated all-core group name removes the ambiguity. - What changed: added `group:core` as the all-built-in-core-tools shorthand; updated `group:openclaw` docs to accurately say "curated OpenClaw integration tools (excludes filesystem, shell runtime, and provider plugins)"; added `expandToolGroups` to the plugin allowlist normalizer so `group:*` entries work in plugin tool policy. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no profile semantics changed. `tools.profile: "full"` still means unrestricted, same as unset. Plugin tools are still not included by `group:core`. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: My OpenClaw runs in a non-admin account with it's own credentials. As such when configuring the browser plugin, I discovered that it had to be explicitly added to tools.allow and that I could allow all future configured plugins with `group:plugins` ```bash openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:plugins"]' --strict-json ``` However, when I did this my agent lost access to all core tools because if tools.allow is present it must specify everything allowed. So I went looking for a group similar to group:plugins and found it missing. Now, with this change full yolo looks like this. ```bash openclaw config set agents.defaults.sandbox.mode off openclaw config set tools.profile full openclaw config set tools.exec.ask off openclaw exec-policy preset yolo openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:core","group:plugins"]' --strict-json ``` - Real environment tested: Mac Mini with openclaw v2026.5.2 built from source ```bash git checkout v2026.5.2 git apply seank-group-core.patch pnpm install pnpm ui:build pnpm build ./scripts/package-mac-app.sh pnpm test src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts pnpm link --global ``` - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:core","group:plugins"]' --strict-json openclaw gateway restart ``` - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Observed result after fix: OpenClaw had access to all core tools as well as browser plugin - What was not tested: I only ran this live on the environment above, beside that just unittest suggested by OpenClaw and GitHub Copilot - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `group:openclaw` was both the only broad group name and a curated subset, creating a naming gap where no group meant "all built-in core tools." - Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted the existence of an all-core group or verified that `group:openclaw` excluded fs/runtime tools. - Contributing context (if known): the naming collision was a design clarity problem, not a runtime regression. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts` - `src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts` - `src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - `group:core` expands to built-in core tools. - Plugin optional tools are not enabled by `group:core` alone, but still work with `group:plugins`. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is pure policy expansion, so unit coverage around the catalog, global tool policy, and plugin allowlist boundary is sufficient. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing group expansion tests covered section groups and `group:openclaw`; this PR adds explicit coverage for `group:core`. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` now support `group:core` for all built-in core tools. - Docs now distinguish: - `tools.profile: "full"`: unrestricted profile, same as unset. - `group:core`: all built-in core tools, excluding plugin tools. - `group:openclaw`: curated OpenClaw integration tools, excluding filesystem, shell runtime, and plugin tools. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user wants broad built-in tool access] -> group:openclaw -> curated subset (misleading docs say "all") After: [user wants all built-in core tools] -> group:core -> all built-in core tools [user wants curated integration tools] -> group:openclaw -> curated subset (docs now accurate) [user wants unrestricted profile] -> tools.profile: full -> unrestricted baseline ``` ## Security Impact (required) - It does not grant anything unless an operator opts into it in tool policy. target.title: fix(googlechat): fix googlechat grant_type
target.body: ## Summary in recent refactor of gaxios, Google chat was broken. #77307 has more details. - Resolves the `unsupported_grant_type` error by ensuring we instantiate the exact same version of `Gaxios` that `google-auth-library` uses internally. - Previously, the dynamic `import('gaxios')` resolved to a hoisted version (6.7.1) in the workspace instead of the version (7.1.4) required by `google-auth-library@10.6.2`. The older version did not natively handle `URLSearchParams`, leading to the body being stringified as JSON (`"{}"`) and the token exchange failing due to missing grant type fields. - Recent refactoring introduced a native `Headers` conversion that broke gaxios 7.x, as it uses direct property assignment (e.g. `headers['Content-Type']`) to prepare form-data bodies. Native `Headers` objects ignore these assignments, causing requests to be sent without the required `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` header. Changes: - Replace native `Headers` conversion in `normalizeGoogleAuthPreparedRequestHeaders` with a compatibility layer that adds `get`/`set`/`has`/`delete` to the plain headers object. - Update `createGoogleAuthFetch` to correctly extract `method`, `headers`, and `body` when called with a native `Request` object. - Add regression test for `Request` property preservation. - Problem: resolves #77307 Google chat was broken in recent refactoring - Why it matters: - What changed: Google chat, headers, - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no change in functionality or other modules ## Change Type (select all) - [x ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #77307 - Related # - [x ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence. target.title: fix(gateway): skip health-monitor restart for terminal WhatsApp disconnects
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: On multi-tenant gateways running ≥1 WhatsApp account, any account that receives a terminal disconnect (`loggedOut` 401, `connectionReplaced` 440) enters a perpetual restart loop — the health monitor calls `startChannel()` on every check interval, each attempt leaks a full Baileys WebSocket + Noise handshake, and over time this exhausts gateway heap (12.9 GB reported over 20 h on 12 accounts), saturates the event loop (~80% CPU), and degrades RPC latency for all tenants on the same gateway (~24 s p99 vs. <200 ms baseline). Recovery requires manual intervention.

- **Root Cause**: Two independent restart paths both lacked a terminal-disconnect guard, and the status surface also missed the signal. (1) `ChannelHealthSnapshot` had no `terminalDisconnect` field, so `evaluateChannelHealth` returned `"not-running"` for every stopped channel regardless of why it stopped, causing the health-monitor to unconditionally call `startChannel()`. (2) The `ChannelManager` task-exit recovery handler fires immediately when the channel task settles — before the health monitor is involved — and only checked `manuallyStopped`, so terminal exits still triggered the backoff-and-restart loop independently. (3) WhatsApp's custom snapshot builder forwarded `healthState` from the runtime snapshot but not `terminalDisconnect`, so `channels.status` recomputed `"not-running"` instead of `"terminal-disconnect"` for logged-out accounts.

- **Fix**: Add `terminalDisconnect?: boolean` to `ChannelHealthSnapshot` and the public `ChannelAccountSnapshot`. The WhatsApp status controller sets the flag to `true` in `markStopped()` when `healthState` is `"logged-out"` or `"conflict"` (the only two states that clear auth and require QR re-pairing), and resets it to `undefined` in `noteConnected()` on successful re-auth. Both restart paths now observe the flag: `evaluateChannelHealth` returns a new `"terminal-disconnect"` reason that the health monitor logs and skips; the `ChannelManager` task-exit handler returns early with an info-level log. The flag is forwarded in both `buildChannelSummary` and `resolveAccountSnapshot.extra` so `channels.status` reflects the correct terminal state. The flag is channel-agnostic: any future extension can set it on its own `ChannelAccountSnapshot` to opt in to the same no-restart behavior without modifying core.

- **What changed**:
  - `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` — added `terminalDisconnect?: boolean` to `ChannelAccountSnapshot`
  - `src/gateway/channel-health-policy.ts` — added field to `ChannelHealthSnapshot`; added `"terminal-disconnect"` reason; added guard before the `not-running` branch
  - `src/gateway/channel-health-monitor.ts` — added skip block for `terminal-disconnect` reason with an info-level log line
  - `src/gateway/server-channels.ts` — added `terminalDisconnect` guard in the task-exit recovery handler after the `manuallyStopped` check
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/types.ts` — added `terminalDisconnect?: boolean` to `WebChannelStatus`
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor-state.ts` — set flag in `markStopped()` for `"logged-out"` / `"conflict"`; clear in `noteConnected()`
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel.ts` — forwarded `terminalDisconnect` from the runtime snapshot in both `buildChannelSummary` and `resolveAccountSnapshot.extra` so `channels.status` correctly surfaces the terminal state
  - `src/gateway/channel-health-policy.test.ts` — 3 new cases covering terminal-disconnect evaluation, no-flag fallthrough, and running-channel flag ignore
  - `src/gateway/channel-health-monitor.test.ts` — 2 new cases proving the monitor skips terminal-disconnect channels and still restarts non-terminal stopped channels
  - `src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts` — 1 new case proving the manager task-exit path does not auto-restart a terminal-disconnect channel
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/channels.status.test.ts` — 2 new cases: `channels.status` reports `"terminal-disconnect"` health state when the flag is set; fallback to account-derived summaries when summary building fails
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor-state.test.ts` — 4 new cases covering `markStopped` sets `terminalDisconnect` for `logged-out`/`conflict`, does not set it for non-terminal states, and `noteConnected` clears it

- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - No changes to reconnect policy, cooldown config, or hourly restart cap
  - No changes to any other channel extension (Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.)
  - `isManuallyStopped()` logic is untouched; manually stopped channels continue to be skipped by the existing guard that runs before health evaluation
  - `resolveChannelRestartReason` is not reachable for `terminal-disconnect` (monitor skips before calling it) and is not modified
  - No new public-facing config keys or gateway events in this change

---

### Reproduction

1. Run a gateway with ≥1 WhatsApp account:
   ```yaml
   channels:
     whatsapp:
       accounts:
         default:
           enabled: true
   ```
2. From the linked phone: Settings → Linked Devices → unlink the session.
3. Observe `gateway.log`:
   ```
   [health-monitor] [whatsapp:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stopped)
   ```
   firing every check interval indefinitely.
4. After this fix:
   ```
   [default] auto-restart skipped, terminal disconnect
   [health-monitor] [whatsapp:default] health-monitor: skipping restart, terminal disconnect
   ```
   fires with no `startChannel()` call, and `channels.status` reports `healthState: "terminal-disconnect"`.

---

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: WhatsApp gateway perpetual restart loop on `loggedOut`/`connectionReplaced` terminal disconnect, causing unbounded WebSocket leak and heap growth.

**Real environment tested**: Unit-level proof via Vitest (no live WhatsApp device available in CI env; Crabbox live validation needed before merge).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/gateway/channel-health-monitor.test.ts src/gateway/channel-health-policy.test.ts src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/channels.status.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor-state.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix**: 322 tests across 12 test files pass, including all 3 new gateway policy cases, 2 monitor cases, 1 manager case, 2 channels.status cases, and 4 monitor-state transition cases.

**Observed result after fix**: `evaluateChannelHealth` returns `"terminal-disconnect"` reason; health monitor and server-channels task-exit handler both skip restart; `channels.status` surfaces `healthState: "terminal-disconnect"`.

**What was not tested**: Live WhatsApp loggedOut/connectionReplaced repro on real device (requires Crabbox with WhatsApp credentials).

---

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: A terminal-disconnect channel will no longer be auto-recovered by either restart path. If `terminalDisconnect` is accidentally set `true` on a channel that could have reconnected, that channel would stay stopped until manually restarted.
- **Mitigation**: The flag is set only for `"logged-out"` and `"conflict"` healthStates, which both clear stored auth credentials — the channel genuinely cannot reconnect without operator QR action. `noteConnected()` unconditionally clears the flag, so a successful re-auth restores normal behavior on all paths. Three policy unit tests, two monitor integration tests, one manager recovery test, two `channels.status` regression tests, and four monitor-state transition tests cover the boundary cases.

---

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

---

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway
- [x] WhatsApp plugin
- [x] Channel status types (additive, backward-compatible)
- [x] Tests

---

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #78419

target.title: feat(msteams): add native plugin interactivity parity
target.body:
## Summary

DO NOT MERGE YET -- for review

- Problem: OpenClaw's Teams channel could send messages/cards, but the native plugin interactivity path still stopped short of Teams. The Codex app-server Teams work therefore had to rely on a command bridge instead of the same core plugin/runtime flow used by first-class channels.
- Why it matters: I am working on this so Teams support for the Codex app-server path is grounded in real OpenClaw channel support, not a partial workaround. Without these core changes, Teams actions, binding flows, message edits, and channel-scoped Graph operations do not have the same native foundation as Discord/Telegram-style plugin integrations.
- What changed: this PR adds native Teams plugin interactive dispatch in `src/plugins/interactive.ts` and `src/plugins/interactive-dispatch-adapters.ts`, adds the Teams runtime surface in `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams.ts`, `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams-typing.ts`, and `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams-ops.runtime.ts`, wires Teams invoke handling through `extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts` and `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler.ts`, extends command/target normalization in `src/plugins/commands.ts` and `src/channels/plugins/target-parsing.ts`, and fixes Teams Graph/edit/allowlist correctness in `extensions/msteams/src/send.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.ts`, and `extensions/msteams/src/policy.ts`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not try to fix unrelated latest-`main` agent/skills TypeScript failures, and it does not claim live-tenant validation for every Teams topology (for example shared-channel behavior still needs real-environment confirmation outside this PR).

## Change Type

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: cross-repo Teams parity work in `openclaw-codex-app-server`
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History

- Root cause: Teams support in core had been built around message/card sending and text-command handling, but not the native plugin interactive dispatch/runtime path. That left Codex app-server Teams support depending on a hidden command bridge instead of the same first-class interactive surface exposed to other channels.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no Teams-native seam/integration coverage for plugin invoke handling, source-message edits/clears, Graph channel id translation, or fail-closed allowlist resolution.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current code shape reflected the earlier minimal Teams MVP, where `src/plugins/interactive.ts` and runtime channel exposure were intentionally limited and Teams only had the smaller send-oriented surface.
- Why this regressed now: the cross-repo Codex Teams work pushed beyond that MVP and exposed the remaining OpenClaw core gaps.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler.plugin-interactions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/send.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/policy.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/interactive.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/commands.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Teams invoke payloads dispatch through the native plugin interactive handler, reply/follow-up/edit/clear flows update the expected Teams message/card surface, command/binding targets normalize consistently, Graph channel operations resolve the correct team/channel ids, and allowlist failures fail closed instead of widening access.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failures crossed the Teams adapter, plugin interactive core, and Graph-backed Teams operations; narrower unit tests would miss the integration seams that were actually broken.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): the files above now cover the main Teams seams.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Teams plugin actions now go through native OpenClaw plugin interactivity instead of relying on a command-only bridge.
- Teams plugin replies/follow-ups/edit-source/clear-actions now preserve better source-message behavior for Adaptive Card flows.
- Teams plugin command binding and explicit target parsing now normalize `teams:` / `msteams:` conversation targets consistently.
- Teams channel-scoped Graph operations use the resolved Graph team id path more safely.
- Teams allowlist resolution now fails closed when primary-channel lookup cannot safely resolve a usable runtime key.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
Teams Action.Submit -> Teams-specific command bridge -> plugin workaround -> partial updates

After:
Teams Action.Submit -> extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts
                  -> src/plugins/interactive.ts
                  -> plugin handler/runtime.channel.msteams
                  -> native reply/edit/typing/channel actions
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This PR gives plugins a new trusted `runtime.channel.msteams` surface for Teams-native operations and adds Teams-native interactive dispatch. The risk is widening what a plugin can do in Teams or routing to the wrong target. Mitigations in this PR are explicit runtime typing in `src/plugins/runtime/types-channel.ts`, targeted runtime wiring instead of broad generic exposure, conversation-scoped interactive handling in `extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts`, Graph team-id resolution fixes in `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.ts` / `extensions/msteams/src/graph.ts`, and fail-closed allowlist behavior in `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.ts` / `extensions/msteams/src/policy.ts`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local `pnpm` workspace checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Microsoft Teams (`extensions/msteams`)
- Relevant config (redacted): local dev config only; no secrets included in this PR

### Steps

1. Enable the Teams channel and a plugin that uses OpenClaw interactive handlers/runtime operations.
2. Trigger a Teams plugin interaction that needs source-message edits, follow-ups, binding, or Teams-native runtime actions.
3. Exercise channel-scoped Teams paths that resolve Graph ids and allowlist-target normalization.

### Expected

- Teams interactive actions route through the native plugin handler path.
- Teams runtime operations are available through `runtime.channel.msteams`.
- Teams message/card edit flows keep the intended source-card behavior.
- Teams channel Graph operations resolve the correct team/channel path.
- Unsafe Teams allowlist fallbacks do not widen access.

### Actual

- This branch implements those paths in code and verifies them with the Teams-focused tests listed below.
- After rebasing onto latest `main`, repo-wide `pnpm check` is blocked by unrelated `src/agents/**` TypeScript failures already present on top of `main`; those are out of scope for this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Concrete issues fixed in this branch and covered by passing tests/review loops:

- Teams plugin interactions could not run through the native OpenClaw interactive dispatch path.
- Teams source-message update paths could clear or duplicate replies incorrectly.
- Teams channel Graph operations could use the wrong team identifier for channel-scoped requests.
- Teams allowlist resolution briefly risked widening access when primary-channel lookup failed; this branch keeps that path fail-closed.

Evidence run locally during this work:

- `pnpm test -- extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.test.ts extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler.plugin-interactions.test.ts extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.test.ts extensions/msteams/src/policy.test.ts`
- Earlier Teams-scoped validation on this branch also included:
  - `extensions/msteams/src/send.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/interactive.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/commands.test.ts`
- Before the rebase to latest `main`, this branch also passed:
  - `pnpm check`
  - `pnpm build`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`

## Human Verification

NOT YET!


- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the Teams interactive dispatch flow from `extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts` into `src/plugins/interactive.ts`
  - verified the new Teams runtime surface and SDK baseline updates
  - verified Teams target parsing / command binding normalization
  - verified Teams Graph id resolution and allowlist behavior through targeted tests and review-fix loops
  - rebased the branch onto latest `origin/main` and resolved the resulting conflicts
- Edge cases checked:
  - Teams source-message edit vs clear-card behavior
  - Teams interaction fallthrough when a handler declines handling
  - Graph team-id recovery / paging behavior
  - fail-closed allowlist resolution when primary-channel lookup is unavailable
- What you did **not** verify:
  - no live Microsoft Teams tenant verification in this PR
  - no shared/private-channel live validation beyond code/test coverage
  - no attempt to fix unrelated latest-`main` `src/agents/**` failures

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Teams interactive/message-edit behavior can differ from Telegram/Discord because Adaptive Cards and Teams invoke semantics are different.
  - Mitigation: keep Teams-specific behavior isolated under `extensions/msteams/**`, cover the adapter seams with tests, and preserve explanatory comments in the non-obvious parsing/Graph-resolution paths.
- Risk: Graph team/channel id translation could still behave differently in real tenants than in mocked tests.
  - Mitigation: the code now handles Graph-usable ids directly, pages through group lookup fallback, and fails closed when it cannot safely resolve a usable target.
- Risk: exposing Teams runtime actions to plugins could widen the trusted plugin surface.
  - Mitigation: the surface is explicit and typed in the runtime channel layer rather than being an unbounded Teams SDK export.

target.title: fix(gateway): add max reconnect limit with opt-in maxReconnectAttempts
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in reconnect attempt limit for GatewayClient to prevent infinite reconnect loops in short-lived clients (gateway CLI, MCP bridge, etc.).

Resolves #45469

## Changes

- Add `maxReconnectAttempts?: number` to `GatewayClientOptions`
- When set, `scheduleReconnect()` gives up after N attempts and fires `onReconnectPaused` with `detailCode: null`
- When unset (default), behavior is unchanged — unlimited retries, preserving node-host recovery semantics
- Reset `reconnectAttempts` counter on successful hello-ok handshake
- Two tests: cap exhaustion + counter reset after recovery

## Why opt-in

Node-host relies on GatewayClient retrying indefinitely during gateway outages. A global cap would zombie node-host processes. Making it opt-in means node-host is unchanged while CLI/bridge callers can opt in to bounded retries.

## Test plan

- [x] Stops after N reconnect attempts and fires onReconnectPaused
- [x] Counter resets on successful connect
- [x] CI green (lint, type-check, security, boundaries)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** GatewayClient reconnects infinitely with no upper bound on attempts. After applying this patch, callers can opt in to a bounded reconnect limit via `maxReconnectAttempts` while node-host retains unbounded retries.

**Real environment tested:** ARM64 Linux (aarch64, Ubuntu), Node.js v22.22.2, running vitest against patched `src/gateway/client.ts` from `fix/max-reconnect-limit-v2` branch.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Ran targeted reconnect tests: `npx vitest run src/gateway/client.test.ts -t "reconnect" --reporter=verbose`

- **Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):**


**Observed result after fix:** With `maxReconnectAttempts: 30` set, the client fires `onReconnectPaused({ code: -1, reason: "max reconnect attempts reached (30)", detailCode: null })` after 30 failures and stops reconnecting. After a successful hello-ok handshake the counter resets, allowing another full cycle. Without the option set, reconnects continue unbounded — no behavior change.

**Not tested:** Full end-to-end reconnect against a live gateway server (no local gateway instance available on this ARM host).
target.author: stellamariesays

target.title: perf(codex): index rollout transcript ids
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Codex native-subagent reconciliation scans every transcript file against every missing child thread id. With many pending children, that turns one filesystem walk into a files-by-children comparison loop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Codex recorder transcripts use the canonical `rollout-<timestamp>-<thread-id>.jsonl` filename. The monitor now extracts that thread id and performs a set lookup, while keeping the existing substring fallback for non-canonical files.

## User Impact

Large Codex session trees reconcile pending native subagents with less CPU work and the same transcript completion behavior.

## Evidence

- Focused tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts`
- Result: 33 tests passed.
- Fresh autoreview: clean, no accepted/actionable findings.
- Codex recorder contract checked in `codex-rs/rollout/src/recorder_tests.rs`.
- Rebased source commit: `e314154269`

target.title: docs(plugins): clarify hook runtime activation
target.body:
## Summary
- document that chat-path hooks such as `before_dispatch` only run when Gateway startup planning actually loads the plugin into the messaging runtime
- tie the hook docs back to the manifest activation contract, especially `activation.onCapabilities: ["hook"]`
- make the non-capability hook-loading rule visible without changing runtime behavior

Closes #77435


## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: The hook docs now describe the actual Gateway startup route for chat-path hooks and no longer present `activation.onChannels` as enough for hook-only runtime loading.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw docs checkout from this PR branch on macOS at head `5c224ebe21eba257ee4734ec8551723f9db788ad`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const s=fs.readFileSync('docs/plugins/hooks.md','utf8'); console.log('old_onChannels_startup_sentence=' + /startup trigger such as[^\n]*onChannels/.test(s)); console.log('hook_capability_manifest_example=' + /\"onCapabilities\": \\[\"hook\"\\]/.test(s));"
  ```
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from a Node docs-source check in the patched checkout:
  ```text
  openclaw_docs_hook_activation=verified
  old_onChannels_startup_sentence=false
  hook_capability_manifest_example=true
  rendered_paragraph=Hook-only or non-capability plugins should declare startup intent explicitly with manifest activation metadata, typically activation.onCapabilities: ["hook"], or through explicit hook-policy config. Do not rely on activation.onChannels alone for hook-only startup.
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The live docs source now points hook-only plugin authors to `activation.onCapabilities: ["hook"]` or explicit hook policy, includes a concrete manifest snippet, and the old `onChannels` startup-trigger sentence is absent.
- What was not tested: The docs page was not deployed from this branch; validation was limited to the local docs source and docs checks.

target.title: feat(plugins): add before_model_call preflight hook
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a typed, fail-closed `before_model_call` hook at Pi, CLI, and Codex app-server model submission boundaries.
- Gates non-bundled plugin conversation access behind `plugins.entries.<id>.hooks.allowConversationAccess`.
- Aligns CLI preflight and `llm_input` with the final prompt after retry selection, transformations, and image shaping, with a 15s default timeout.

## Changes

- `src/plugins/*` — hook contract, runner default timeout/fail-closed semantics, conversation gating compatibility record.
- `src/agents/*` — shared harness hook helper, Pi/CLI wiring, session-expiry retry coverage, and model-auth fixture compatibility with current `@mariozechner/pi-ai` types.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/*` — Codex preflight before `turn/start`.
- `docs/*`, generated config/API baselines, and changelog updated for the new hook.

## Testing

target.title: diag(cli-runner): attribute AbortError trigger source
target.body:
## Problem

`[agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn failed: ... error=AbortError` log lines have no detail field, so operators cannot tell which of four distinct trigger paths fired:

1. User-typed abort word (`stop`, `wait`, etc. via `ABORT_TRIGGERS`)
2. `abortForRestart` from compaction-restart / drain
3. Mid-turn `/model` switch (`requestLiveSessionModelSwitch`)
4. Explicit `chat.abort` RPC

This makes the bug pattern impossible to fix without speculation. We've been seeing 20+ AbortError lines / 10 days across our deployment with no way to attribute them.

## Change

Diagnostic enrichment only — no behavior change.

- `src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts`:
  - `createAbortError(reason?)` now accepts and includes the upstream abort reason in the error message (was: zero-arg, fixed `"CLI run aborted"`).
  - `failTurn` appends `detail="..."` (truncated to 240 chars) to the log line.
  - The two abort paths split: `abortFromSignal` reads `params.context.params.abortSignal.reason` (preserves `controller.abort(reason)` payload — `UserAbortError` for stop-words, `Error("Reply operation aborted for restart")` for compaction, etc.); `abortFromReplyBackend` tags `replyBackend:<reason>` (`user_abort`, `restart`, or `superseded`).
- `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts`: same `createCliAbortError(reason?)` upgrade for the non-live CLI path.
- `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts`: `abortChatRunById` calls `controller.abort("chat-abort:<stopReason>")` instead of `.abort()`, so the existing `stopReason` (e.g., `"rpc"`, `"stop"`, `"timeout"`) propagates.

## Effect on log lines

Before:
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError
\`\`\`

After (one of):
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted by user"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted for restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: replyBackend:restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: chat-abort:rpc"
\`\`\`

That is sufficient to attribute each abort to its real source (operator stop-word vs. compaction restart vs. model switch vs. RPC) without re-investigating from scratch.

## Tests

- New: `src/agents/cli-runner/abort-reason.test.ts` — 7 unit tests covering string / Error / object / nullish abort-reason inputs for both `createAbortError` (live-session path) and `createCliAbortError` (non-live path). All pass.
- Existing: `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts` 4/4 pass; `src/agents/cli-runner/` 35/35 pass.
- `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.test.json` both clean.

`pnpm check:changed` not run — would be appreciated as part of CI verification.

## Out of scope

I deliberately did NOT raise the abort grace timeout, change `ABORT_TRIGGERS`, or alter compaction-restart policy. Without trigger evidence those would be guesses. This PR is the prerequisite — once a few new aborts arrive with attributed reasons, the actual fix becomes obvious.

## Provenance

This change was scoped and authored by a Claude Code background agent during a source-level diagnostic of multiple gateway issues. Full investigation writeup (root causes, failing call paths, what wasn't fixed and why) is in `DIAGNOSTIC.md` on the branch but not included in the diff so it doesn't pollute the repo.

target.title: feat(tools/write): add append mode for agent writes
target.body:
## Summary

Revives and rebases the closed append-mode work from #75549 for the canonical data-loss issue #40001.

The `write` tool currently overwrites existing files. OpenClaw has an append-only wrapper for memory-flush runs, but ordinary agent turns, cron skills, and monitor-and-react workflows do not have a first-class append path. This PR adds a backwards-compatible optional `append: true` parameter to `write` so agents can append without synthesizing a read-concatenate-overwrite sequence.

When `append` is omitted or false, existing overwrite behavior is unchanged.

## What changed

- Adds optional `append?: boolean` handling for host/workspace and sandbox write operations.
- Preserves existing workspace/root guard behavior.
- Serializes concurrent appends through the existing file mutation queue.
- Preserves validation behavior for blank paths and invalid content.
- Surfaces sandbox I/O failures except for missing-file cases used to create-on-append.
- Honors aborted append calls before entering the mutation queue.
- Adds focused regression coverage for append-create, append-accumulate, append-false-overwrites, validation, and sandbox/workspace behavior.

## Validation

Run in an isolated checkout on current `origin/main`:

- `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.params.test.ts`
  - passed: `10 files / 170 tests`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm lint:core`
  - passed: `0 warnings / 0 errors`

## Notes

This PR intentionally keeps the API small and backwards-compatible. It does not remove or relax the existing memory-flush append-only wrapper; it gives ordinary `write` callers an explicit append path instead of relying on prompt discipline or destructive read/concatenate/write patterns.

Fixes #40001.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** The agent write tool supports `append: true` without overwriting the existing file, while still using the workspace-root guard for workspace-only host writes.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux using the actual `createHostWorkspaceWriteTool` implementation against a temporary workspace on the real filesystem.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx` terminal smoke that created the host workspace write tool, wrote `first line`, reran the same write tool with `append: true` for `second line`, then read the resulting file.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the smoke:

  ```text
  workspace: $TMPDIR/openclaw-write-append-proof-.../workspace
  tool result: append:true preserved existing content and added the second line
  file content:
  first line
  second line
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The final file retained the original content and appended the new content in order; the append path did not truncate or replace the file.
- **What was not tested:** No live Gateway session was exercised; validation used the real tool implementation in an isolated local checkout plus targeted regression/type checks.

target.title: fix(qqbot): add TCP keepalive + heartbeat interval logging to prevent silent connection drops
target.body:
### Problem

**Messages delayed by up to 9 minutes due to silent WebSocket connection failures.**

On 2026-05-04 07:13, a user sent a message via QQBot. The message was not received until 07:22:18 — **9 minutes of delay**. During this entire period, the OpenClaw gateway showed no errors, no reconnection attempts, and no heartbeat failures. The connection appeared healthy but was in fact dead.

**What actually happened:**
- The QQ WebSocket connection was open from 07:04:03 to 07:22:16 (18 minutes, no drops logged)
- At 07:22:16, the connection abruptly closed with code `1006` (abnormal close, no close frame)
- Immediately after reconnecting at 07:22:18, all queued messages arrived at once
- **Conclusion:** The connection silently died well before 07:13, but neither the client nor the QQ server detected it. Messages were buffered server-side and only delivered after the client reconnect.

**Why no detection?** The existing QQBot gateway implementation has two gaps:
1. **No TCP keepalive** — The `ws` library was instantiated without `setKeepAlive`, meaning the OS-level TCP connection is not probed. Network middleboxes (routers, NAT, firewalls) silently drop idle connections without sending RST/FIN.
2. **No heartbeat timeout detection** — If the server sends a `HEARTBEAT_ACK` on time, the client assumes the connection is healthy. But if the `HEARTBEAT_ACK` is lost in transit (because the underlying TCP path is dead), the client never notices. There is no timeout or ACK validation.

**Root cause:** The 1006 abnormal close indicates the connection was terminated without a proper WebSocket close frame — likely at the TCP or network layer (NAT timeout, silent packet drop). The client had no way to detect this before receiving a message, because there was no lower-level keepalive probe and no heartbeat ACK timeout.

### Solution

**Three changes in `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/gateway-connection.ts`:**

#### 1. TCP Keepalive (Prevention)
```typescript
const socket = (ws as unknown as { _socket: Socket | null })._socket;
if (socket) {
  socket.setKeepAlive(true, 30_000);  // probe every 30s
  socket.setTimeout(60_000);          // 60s with no data → timeout
  socket.on("timeout", () => {
    log?.warn?.(`WebSocket socket timeout — closing and reconnecting`);
    ws.close();  // triggers existing reconnect flow
  });
}
```

- `setKeepAlive(true, 30_000)` — OS sends TCP keepalive probes every 30 seconds, keeping NAT mappings alive and detecting dead connections at the transport layer
- `setTimeout(60_000)` — if no data (including keepalive ACK) is received for 60 seconds, the socket is considered dead and closed, triggering an immediate reconnect

#### 2. Heartbeat Interval Logging (Debugging)
```typescript
const interval = d.heartbeat_interval;
this.ctx.log?.info(`Heartbeat interval: ${interval}ms (${(interval / 1000).toFixed(1)}s)`);
```

- Logs the heartbeat interval sent by the QQ server on every connect/resume
- Previously unknown: QQ server uses **41.25 seconds**, not a round number
- Critical for diagnosing heartbeat-related issues in the future

#### 3. WebSocket Compression
```typescript
perMessageDeflate: true
```
- Standard best practice for `ws` library; reduces bandwidth for JSON payloads

### Verification

- **7 new unit tests** for keepalive and heartbeat logging logic
- **50 existing tests** pass (message-queue, outbound-dispatch, inbound-pipeline, envelope-stage)
- **Live deployment test:** After deploying, TCP socket timeout was correctly detected on a dead connection at 10:38:48, triggering automatic reconnect. Heartbeat interval logged as `41250ms (41.3s)`.

### Expected Impact

- **Before:** Dead connections go undetected for minutes. Users see message delays of 5-10 minutes. Only a manual restart or the next server-side close triggers reconnect.
- **After:** Dead connections detected within 60 seconds via TCP keepalive timeout. Automatic reconnect restores message delivery. Heartbeat interval visible in logs for troubleshooting.

target.title: fix(cli): exit after hooks inspection output
target.body:
## Summary

- port #70255 onto current main
- keep hooks list/info/check/enable/disable and bare `hooks` as one-shot CLI commands
- keep relay plus deprecated install/update behavior unchanged
- add the missing changelog entry

## Why

Plugin diagnostics can load third-party hook plugins. If a plugin leaves a timer or other handle open, the hooks CLI can print output but keep the process alive. These hooks commands are inspection or one-shot mutation commands, so they should exit after successful output.

Supersedes #70255.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts src/plugins/status.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/cli/hooks-cli.ts src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: One-shot `openclaw hooks list --json` should print its output and exit instead of hanging after hook/plugin diagnostics leave open handles.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and the real CLI path, not a unit test.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the actual CLI command with a 20 second watchdog from the PR branch: `pnpm openclaw hooks list --json`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
$ python3 watchdog-wrapper-for pnpm openclaw hooks list --json
exit:0
stdout_bytes:273
stdout_head:
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-hooks-cli-exit-refresh
> node scripts/run-node.mjs hooks list --json

{
  "workspaceDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/workspace",
  "managedHooksDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/hooks",
  "hooks": []
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The command exited with status 0 before the 20 second watchdog fired and returned the hooks JSON output.
- What was not tested: No separate hanging third-party plugin was installed for this proof. The focused regression test still covers the lingering-handle path.

target.title: fix(discord): preserve target intent during normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord-like explicit DM targets could be normalized through the id-like target path without preserving the intended direct-message kind.
- Why it matters: A Discord target that is syntactically a user/direct target should stay routed as a user/direct target instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What changed: Shared target-kind inference now lives in `target-normalization`, and both outbound target resolution and cron delivery pass the inferred kind into id-like plugin resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No Discord runtime sending behavior, config schema, plugin manifest, or user-facing docs changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Explicit Discord direct-message targets such as `user:<id>` preserve inferred user/direct intent when id-like target resolution runs for outbound and cron delivery.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows/PowerShell, Discord plugin configured with a real bot token and a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran local OpenClaw Discord outbound/cron delivery paths against an explicit `user:<id>` Discord target using the patched branch, then compared the live delivery result with the new resolver/cron coverage.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Existing live-result report from @AScriver after the local Discord run, with private IDs and tokens redacted:

```text
Scenario: Discord outbound/cron delivery to an explicit user:<id> target
Result: delivered to the intended DM recipient and preserved explicit user target intent instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference
Coverage alignment: matches the targeted resolver and cron tests added in this PR
```

- Observed result after fix: Discord delivered to the intended DM recipient, and the explicit user/direct target intent was preserved instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What was not tested: Raw Discord token and private IDs are not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Source-level before-state showed the id-like resolver did not receive inferred `preferredKind` for outbound/cron paths; the targeted regression tests cover that missing handoff.
## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: id-like target resolution received only the raw target string, so the plugin resolver could lose the caller's inferred direct-message intent after normalization.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered id-like fallback and directory inference separately, but not propagation of inferred DM/user kind into plugin id-like resolution.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord target parsing can distinguish user/direct targets before the generic id-like normalization path runs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: inferred direct-message/user kind is passed into id-like plugin target resolution.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver seam and cron delivery call path without needing a live Discord bot.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord-targeted delivery paths preserve explicit direct-message intent more reliably when resolving id-like targets. No config or command syntax changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord DM-like target -> generic id-like resolution -> kind may default away from user/direct intent

After:
Discord DM-like target -> infer user/direct kind -> id-like resolution receives preferredKind=user -> direct target preserved
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows, PowerShell
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout, Node/pnpm from workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord target routing logic
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Resolve a plugin id-like target whose channel messaging adapter infers `direct` chat type.
2. Resolve a cron delivery target for an explicit DM-like target.
3. Assert the id-like resolver receives `preferredKind: "user"` and preserves the returned target.

### Expected

- The id-like plugin resolver receives the inferred user/direct kind.
- Cron delivery preserves explicit DM intent after target normalization.

### Actual

- Verified by the new focused tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted validation:

```bash
pnpm test src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts
pnpm check:changed
```

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local Discord outbound/cron delivery to a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target was reported after this patch; target resolver and cron delivery target unit/seam coverage also passed locally.
- Edge cases checked: id-like raw targets continue passing `preferredKind: "group"`; explicit DM-like targets pass `preferredKind: "user"`.
- What I did **not** verify: raw Discord token/private IDs are intentionally not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: shared target kind inference could affect non-Discord channels that expose `inferTargetChatType`.
  - Mitigation: the helper preserves existing fallback behavior, maps `channel` to `group` where directory lookup expects groups, and adds focused coverage for the new id-like propagation path.

target.title: fix(plugins): supplement ClawHub artifact metadata
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept current ClawHub artifact resolver responses where `artifact` is the package artifact summary shape.
- When the resolver response has a legacy zip artifact but no archive verification metadata, load the version metadata and use its `sha256hash` or `files[]` for verification.
- Add a regression test for the current `clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9` response shape.

## Why

The ClawHub artifact endpoint can return a legacy zip artifact summary while the install verification metadata is only present on the version endpoint. OpenClaw rejected that package as missing verification metadata, even though the package was valid and installable.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts && git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real-world validation

- `HOME=$tmp/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$tmp/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$tmp/openclaw.json pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9 --dangerously-force-unsafe-install`

Yes. I used Codex and reviewed the changed code and tests.

- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, installing the live `clawhub:zeroapi` package.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the real CLI install path from the PR branch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force`.

target.title: fix: prune all history images when current turn has fresh attachments
target.body:
## Problem

When sending a new image for analysis, stale image data from previous conversation turns can contaminate the model's interpretation of the current image. Reported in #66702.

## Solution

- Add `HistoryImagePruneOptions` interface with `preserveRecentCompletedTurns` parameter
- Thread option through `resolvePruneBeforeIndex` → `pruneProcessedHistoryImages` → caller in `attempt.ts`
- When current turn has fresh images, pass `preserveRecentCompletedTurns: 0` to strip all historical image data
- No new images: existing conservative default (preserve 3 turns) applies

Backward-compatible. Existing tests unchanged.

Fixes #66702

target.title: Fix/cron duplicate name validation
target.body:
Fixes #76160

\`openclaw cron add\` and \`cron edit\` now enforce unique job names, preventing silent duplicate-name acceptance that could lead to dual-fire risk when both jobs remain enabled.

## Problem

Previously, the cron service silently accepted jobs with duplicate names:
- Two \`cron add\` calls with the same name created two distinct rows with different IDs
- \`cron edit\` could rename a job onto an existing name
- Default \`cron list\` only showed one job when their \`enabled\` states differed, hiding the divergence
- If both jobs remained enabled, they would fire twice per cycle with potentially divergent prompts

## Solution

Added name uniqueness validation in the service layer:
- **\`cron.add\`**: Checks for existing job with the same name before creating
- **\`cron.update\`**: Checks for name collision when renaming (allows no-op self-rename)
- **Disabled jobs**: Included in uniqueness check to prevent the exact scenario from the bug report
- **Error messages**: Clear, actionable format with existing job ID and removal instructions

## Implementation

- \`src/cron/service/ops.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` checks in \`add()\` and \`update()\`
- \`src/cron/service/jobs.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` helper function
- \`src/cron/service.duplicate-names.test.ts\`: Comprehensive test suite (13 test cases)

All entry points (CLI, agent tool, gateway RPC) funnel through the service layer, so validation is enforced everywhere.

## Testing

Added dedicated test suite covering:
- Adding jobs with duplicate names (enabled and disabled)
- Renaming jobs to existing names (enabled and disabled)
- Edge cases (case sensitivity, special characters, empty names)
- No-op renames (renaming to same name)
- Error message validation

## Breaking Changes

None. This is a bug fix that prevents invalid state. Operators who were accidentally creating duplicate names will now receive clear error messages with instructions to resolve the conflict.

## Related

- Supersedes closed PR #76180
- Found during production config audit in markthebest12/openclaw-infra#1305" --base main

target.title: feat(openai): dynamic model catalog discovery from upstream /v1/models
target.body:
First slice of the design proposed in #74481 — adds the discovery primitive without yet wiring it into catalog resolution. Keeping infrastructure and integration in separate PRs so the cache strategy / response mapping can be reviewed independently of the bigger plumbing question (where in the model-resolution path discovery should be invoked, merge-vs-replace semantics, Control UI refresh trigger).

## What this PR does

Adds `extensions/openai/discovery.ts` exposing `discoverOpenAIModels({baseUrl, apiKey, fetchFn?, now?})`:

- Fetches `${baseUrl}/models` with `Authorization: Bearer ${apiKey}`.
- Maps the response (strict superset of OpenAI's `/v1/models` envelope — gateways like AceTeam ship extra fields like `context_window`, `max_output_tokens`, `modalities`, `cost_per_million_tokens` and we honor them when present).
- Falls back to per-id heuristics (default 128k context, 16k max output, text-only, zero cost; `reasoning=true` for `gpt-5.x` / `o[1,3,4]` families).
- Caches per `(baseUrl, last-8-of-apiKey)` for 1h. Rotating the key invalidates.
- Fail-soft: returns last-known-good on transient failure, returns `[]` when there's no cache and the fetch fails. Never throws at the caller.

## Mirrors existing precedent

This is the same shape as `extensions/amazon-bedrock-mantle/discovery.ts:265` — same TTL, same cache-on-success / cache-on-failure semantics, same `ModelDefinitionConfig[]` return type. Makes it familiar to anyone who has read that file.

## What this PR does NOT do (intentionally)

- **Doesn't wire discovery into catalog resolution.** The static catalog in `openai-provider.ts` is unchanged. To exercise the discovered list you'd call `discoverOpenAIModels()` from your own integration today; a follow-up PR will plumb it into the dynamic-model resolution path.
- **Doesn't touch the `ProviderPlugin` interface.** The proposed `resolveDynamicCatalog` hook in #74481 needs maintainer agreement on shape before it lands. If reviewers prefer that path, this discovery function is what the OpenAI plugin's `resolveDynamicCatalog` would call.
- **Doesn't address Anthropic.** Anthropic's `/v1/models` shape and auth header conventions differ; that's a separate PR mirroring this one.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/openai/discovery.test.ts` → 12/12 pass.
- [x] Coverage: missing inputs, header/URL shape, trailing-slash normalization, AceTeam superset fields, modality/cost/reasoning mapping, sort, cache hit, TTL expiry, transient-failure fallback, network-error fallback, no-cache failure path.
- [ ] Integration: hit a real `/v1/models` endpoint and confirm the mapped output is sensible.

## Discussion

Design questions (from #74481) still open:
1. Hook name/shape for plumbing into catalog resolution.
2. Merge-vs-replace semantics — does dynamic discovery need its own setting, or does `models.modelCatalogMode` cover it?
3. Cache scoping for multi-tenant gateways where the same `baseUrl` returns different lists per org.

Happy to revise this PR or split further once those land.

## Forks affected

`aceteam-ai/safeclaw` (OpenClaw + AEP safety-proxy fork) needs this for its model picker to reflect what its proxy actually accepts. Will consume the same discovery function.
