target.title: Fix delivery-ready verification blockers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR closes the remaining delivery-ready verification blockers for the current branch.

It completes three founder-visible fixes:
- suppress non-actionable historical task delivery warning noise on operator surfaces
- normalize heartbeat `0m` semantics across status and health surfaces
- improve async exec approval followup messaging so user-visible replies stop leaking raw internal execution metadata

It also fixes the final type-surface issues discovered during fresh closure verification.

## What changed

### Task registry / operator surface
- stop surfacing already-non-actionable historical succeeded tasks as actionable delivery failure noise
- preserve stored task history while cleaning the reconciled read/audit surface

### Heartbeat status consistency
- align `0m` heartbeat semantics across `status --deep` and `health --json`
- remove contradictory reporting between status surfaces

### Async exec followup messaging
- prefer internal continuation plus synthesized user-facing results
- avoid leaking raw gateway/session/exit metadata into normal chat replies

### Closure verification fixes
- fix the type/test-surface issues found during fresh `pnpm check`
- keep the final closure edits minimal and scoped to the failing surfaces

## Verification

Fresh verification on this branch:
- `pnpm lint` ✅
- `pnpm check` ✅
- `pnpm build` ✅

Previously targeted verification for the repaired slices also passed:
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/health.command.coverage.test.ts src/commands/status-all/diagnosis.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts test/release-check.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` ✅

## Notes

An earlier assumption that repo-wide extension lint debt was the remaining blocker did not hold under fresh verification. The real final blockers were a small set of type-surface failures, which are fixed here.

- What changed: this PR adds QR login result codes to the shared adapter surface, propagates them through the WhatsApp tool/runtime wrappers, and adds the gateway preflight hook that WhatsApp uses before stop/start.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not yet add existing-QR reuse ahead of preflight; that stays in the next PR.

target.title: fix(cron): emit before_agent_reply phase unconditionally for cron triggers
target.body:
## Problem

Cron isolated-agent turns can abort with `externalAbort: true`, `stopReason: "aborted"`, and `error: "LLM request failed."` despite the model provider being healthy and no rate-limit hit. The abort fires after \~6 minutes, well within the configured `timeoutSeconds: 600` (10 min) wall clock.

See #93530 for full issue details.

## Root Cause

The cron watchdog (`createCronAgentWatchdog` in `src/cron/service/agent-watchdog.ts`) depends on the `before_agent_reply` execution phase to transition into the "executing" stage and clear its pre-execution watchdog timer.

In `runEmbeddedAgentInternal` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts`), the `before_agent_reply` phase was only emitted when `hookRunner?.hasHooks("before_agent_reply")` returned true. When no hook plugin registered for that event:

1. `runEmbeddedAgent` enters the cron-trigger branch
2. `hookRunner?.hasHooks("before_agent_reply")` returns false → phase is skipped
3. The watchdog never transitions out of "waiting_for_execution"
4. Pre-execution timeout fires (default: 60s)
5. `triggerTimeout()` → `runAbortController.abort(reason)`
6. The on-going LLM call is interrupted → `externalAbort: true` → "LLM request failed."

## Fix

This PR unconditionally emits the `before_agent_reply` phase for cron triggers, then separately wraps the hook execution in the `hasHooks` guard. This way the watchdog sees the phase even when no before_agent_reply hook is registered.

**Before:** `if (trigger === "cron" && hookRunner?.hasHooks("before_agent_reply"))`
**After:** `if (trigger === "cron")` (emits phase) + `if (hookRunner?.hasHooks("before_agent_reply"))` (runs hook)

## Testing

- Existing cron tests pass unchanged since the phase is now emitted strictly *before* the hook guard that was already there
- The watchdog pre-execution timeout path is exercised by `src/cron/service/agent-watchdog.test.ts`
- Manual verification: _pending_

Fixes #93530

target.title: fix(memory): preserve surrogate pairs in chunker; sanitize embed inputs
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix the outer coarse-split loop in `chunkMarkdown` so it does not bisect a UTF-16 surrogate pair (root cause of indexing stalls).
- Add a defensive `stripUnpairedSurrogates` sanitizer at the embed boundary (the safety net #27753 originally requested).
- Tests for both, including a new regression test that triggers the exact code path the existing surrogate-pair test missed.

Closes #27753.
Fixes #65782.

## Why

Memory indexing stalls permanently for any agent whose content contains an emoji or supplementary-plane character that the chunker happens to split across a surrogate-pair boundary. `openclaw memory status` reports the index as `clean / no issues` while ~98% of sessions are unindexed.

End-to-end symptom (verbose):

```
[memory] embeddings: batch start  (×N)
Memory index failed (<agent>): openai embeddings failed: 500
{"error":{"message":"... 'utf-8' codec can't encode character '\\ud83c'
in position 3229: surrogates not allowed ..."}}
```

Two failure modes compound:

1. **Chunker** (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`) — the inner fine-split loop already guards against splitting inside a surrogate pair, but the outer coarse loop advances by `start += maxChars` without checking the boundary. When the cut lands inside e.g. an emoji like 🌸 (`U+1F338` = `\uD83C\uDF38`), the chunk ends with a lone high surrogate and the next begins with a lone low surrogate. The existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) does not catch this because it uses an all-surrogate-pair line — the high `estimateStringChars` weight forces the inner pass, which is correct.
2. **Embedding HTTP path** — Python JSON encoders (e.g. via LiteLLM proxies) reject lone surrogates with a 500 wrapping a `UnicodeEncodeError`. The retry policy only matches `rate_limit | 429 | 5\d\d | cloudflare | tokens per day`, but the body shape is a connect-error, not a transient one — and the indexer is transactional, so a single bad batch rolls back the entire run. Every subsequent `openclaw memory index` invocation reproduces the same failure.

## What changed

### `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`

```diff
- for (let start = 0; start < line.length; start += maxChars) {
-   const coarse = line.slice(start, start + maxChars);
+ for (let start = 0; start < line.length; ) {
+   let coarseEnd = Math.min(line.length, start + maxChars);
+   // Avoid splitting inside a UTF-16 surrogate pair (emoji, CJK Extension B+).
+   if (coarseEnd < line.length) {
+     const lastCode = line.charCodeAt(coarseEnd - 1);
+     if (lastCode >= 0xd800 && lastCode <= 0xdbff) {
+       coarseEnd += 1; // include the trailing low surrogate
+     }
+   }
+   const coarse = line.slice(start, coarseEnd);
+   start = coarseEnd;
```

Mirrors the existing inner-loop guard at the same file/line. New regression test (`"does not break surrogate pairs at the coarse split boundary (issue #27753)"`) constructs a line of `39 ASCII + 🌸 + 39 ASCII` so the outer cut at `start + maxChars = 40` lands between the high and low surrogate. Without the fix, the test fails by producing chunks containing lone surrogates.

### `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-ops.ts`

Adds a tiny exported helper:

```ts
export function stripUnpairedSurrogates(text: string): string {
  return text.replace(
    /[\uD800-\uDBFF](?![\uDC00-\uDFFF])|(?<![\uD800-\uDBFF])[\uDC00-\uDFFF]/g,
    "\uFFFD",
  );
}
```

…and applies it at the entry of `embedBatchWithRetry` and `embedBatchInputsWithRetry`. This is the change #27753 originally requested. It protects against any other code path that might produce a lone surrogate (LLM tool output, file ingestion, future chunkers) — chunker-only fix is necessary but not sufficient.

Adds `manager-embedding-ops.test.ts` with focused unit tests for the helper (preserves valid pairs, replaces lone high/low surrogates, handles reversed pairs, no-op on empty/non-string).

## Test plan

- [x] Verified live on installed runtime (`openclaw 2026.4.5`) by patching the equivalent bundled artifact under `dist/manager-CKYnEo0k.js` and running `openclaw memory index --agent <id>`:

  | | before | after |
  |---|---:|---:|
  | clawdea files | 6 | **166** |
  | clawdea chunks | 93 | **4584** |
  | dev files | 2 | **17** |
  | dev chunks | 251 | **588** |

  `openclaw memory search` works end-to-end after the fix.
- [ ] CI runs `pnpm test` against the new unit tests in `extensions/memory-core` and `packages/memory-host-sdk`.
- [ ] Existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) still passes.

## Notes for reviewers

- I did not run `pnpm install` locally to execute vitest — the runtime fix is verified live, and the new tests follow the same vitest patterns as adjacent files (`manager-embedding-cache.test.ts`, `internal.test.ts`).
- The retry-policy gap (`fetch failed` / `UnicodeEncodeError` 500 not retried) is a separate concern tracked in #56815 / #58255 — out of scope for this PR. With the chunker + sanitizer in place, the original failure no longer reaches the retry path.
- If the team prefers the helper to live in a shared utility module (e.g. under `packages/memory-host-sdk`) rather than `manager-embedding-ops.ts`, happy to relocate.

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.
- Ran `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: Copied local command output from the after-patch run:

```text
format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts: passed
pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:
  src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
  src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
  src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts
  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts
result: 314 assertions passed
```

Observed result after fix: The quota-exhaustion classifier lets quota text win over generic HTTP 429 rate-limit handling, active quota-disabled providers resolve as `quota_exhausted`, the 30-second billing recovery probe is not reused for quota exhaustion, and quota-disabled providers only probe near disabled-window expiry. The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.

target.title: fix: accept valid MCP tool schemas without strict inputSchema.type check
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes MCP server loading failure when tools have `inputSchema` without explicit `type: "object"`.

Closes #63602

## Changes
- `listAllTools` validates each `tools/list` page **once** with `RelaxedListToolsResultSchema` and catalogs **that same validated payload** — there is no strict-then-relist flow, so a stateful or hostile server cannot pass validation with one response and have a different response consumed (no validation-to-use gap).
- `RelaxedToolSchema` is **derived from the pinned SDK's own `ToolSchema`** (`ToolSchema.extend` / `ToolSchema.shape.inputSchema.extend`) and differs from it at exactly one point: the root `inputSchema.type` is an optional string instead of `z.literal("object")`. Every other field — `outputSchema`, task `execution` metadata, `annotations`, pagination — keeps the exact pinned SDK contract, so malformed metadata still rejects the page.
- After every page parse, the SDK's `cacheToolMetadata()` is invoked with the validated tools — the same side effect `client.listTools()` runs — so `callTool()` enforces output-schema validation and task-required guards identically to the SDK flow. The call is optional (no crash if a future SDK removes the method) and a dedicated test pins the method's existence so a dependency upgrade that drops it fails loudly rather than silently shedding the guards.
- `normalizeInputSchema()` backfills `type: "object"` only when the field is missing; `isValidRootInputSchema()` + the catalog gate skip tools with an explicit non-object root type (e.g. `"string"`, `"array"`) with a `logWarn`. Server `toolCount` reports accepted tools only.

## Root Cause
The MCP SDK v1.29.0 `ToolSchema` validates `inputSchema.type` as `z.literal("object")`. Some MCP servers (especially older SDKs or custom implementations) omit `type` from `inputSchema`, returning `{ properties: { ... } }`, which trips a Zod validation error: `invalid_value expected "object" in inputSchema.type` and crashes the whole catalog.

## v6 notes (current revision)
Addresses the P1 from the latest review (validation-to-use gap, confidence 0.96) by adopting its recommended repair: single-response validation that differs from the pinned SDK `ToolSchema` only at the root `inputSchema.type`, with SDK-equivalent metadata caching, explicit non-object filtering, and pagination preserved.
- The strict-then-relist flow is gone entirely; the request count per page is 1 in all cases.
- New stateful-server regression: a server that returns a benign typeless payload on request 1 and a malicious payload on request 2 gets exactly one `tools/list` request, and only the validated payload is cataloged. **Mutation-checked**: against the previous strict-then-relist implementation this test fails with the catalog containing the drifted (never-strictly-validated) tool — reproducing the reported attack — and the server log showing two list requests.
- New SDK-seam pin test: `Client.prototype.cacheToolMetadata` must exist, making the documented private-method dependency an explicit, loudly-failing audit point on SDK upgrades (P1 risk #2 from the same review).
- Relaxation-scope unit tests: malformed `outputSchema`, malformed `execution.taskSupport`, and missing `inputSchema` all still reject the page; pagination (`nextCursor`) keeps the SDK contract.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Bundle MCP tool cataloging accepts tools whose root `inputSchema` omits `type` by validating each `tools/list` response once against a schema derived from the pinned SDK contract (relaxed only at the root `inputSchema.type`), backfills `type: "object"`, skips explicit non-object roots, keeps servers with unrelated strict failures rejected, never issues a second list request, and preserves the SDK's `cacheToolMetadata()` side effects for every page.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS from the rebased PR head, Node 25.2.1, pnpm 11.2.2, driving the real bundle MCP runtime (`getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime` → `getCatalog()` → `callTool()`) against four real out-of-process stdio MCP servers, one of them stateful (different payload per request).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm test src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, and `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` on both files, plus a live four-server e2e script via `pnpm exec tsx`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  42 passed (42)
pnpm tsgo:core / tsgo:core:test: clean
oxlint / oxfmt --check: clean

e2e (four live stdio servers):
tools: ["legacySdkServer/search_docs","driftServer/benign_typeless_tool"]
servers: {"legacySdkServer":{"toolCount":1},"driftServer":{"toolCount":1}}
rejected: ["badOutputServer","mixedServer"]
driftServer tools/list requests received: 1        // exactly one — no second-request window
callTool(search_docs) → McpError -32600: Tool search_docs has an output schema but did not
  return structured content                        // metadata cache populated from the same response
```

Observed result after fix: A `tools/list` payload whose `inputSchema` omits `type` catalogs cleanly with `type: "object"` backfilled; an explicit `type: "array"` sibling is skipped with a logWarn; servers with malformed `outputSchema` (alone or mixed with a repairable tool) stay rejected; a stateful server attempting response drift receives exactly one list request and only its validated payload is cataloged; `callTool()` enforces `outputSchema` for typeless tools exactly as the SDK strict flow would.

What was not tested: A live third-party MCP server connected to an actual model-provider tool call was not tested in this environment.

target.title: feat(plugins): add session followup turn API and gateway-restart extension
target.body:
## Summary

Add a new `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()` method to the plugin runtime, enabling plugins to schedule proactive agent turns for any session — including cold sessions with no active user interaction.

> **Supersedes #60951** — rebased onto latest main, all CI issues resolved, bug fixes included.

## Problem

Plugins have no way to inject a user-role message into an existing session and trigger an agent turn. The available primitives (`enqueueSystemEvent`, `subagent.run`) either don't initiate turns or create new sessions instead of continuing the original conversation. This blocks use cases like:

- **Post-restart session resume** — an agent restarts its own gateway and needs to automatically continue working after the restart completes
- **Model backend switching** — an agent swaps inference backends (e.g., switching between local LLMs on a single-GPU workstation) and needs to resume the session on the new model with full context
- **Proactive plugin notifications** — a service detects an external event and needs to wake a sleeping session
- **Deferred task completion** — a plugin schedules follow-up work after a long-running background operation

## Solution

### Core API: `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()`

A new `followup` namespace on `PluginRuntimeCore` that:

1. **Reconstructs a full `FollowupRun`** from the session store via `buildFollowupRunForSession()` — resolving agent identity, workspace, provider/model, session file, and delivery context from the persisted `SessionEntry`.

2. **Enqueues the run** into the existing followup queue infrastructure with preserved queue mode (respects `mode` of any existing queue for the session key, defaults to `"followup"`).

3. **Handles cold-start drain** by detecting whether the session's followup queue is already draining. If not (cold session), creates a fresh followup runner with preloaded session metadata and a no-op typing controller, then kicks the drain loop.

```typescript
// Plugin usage
const enqueued = await api.runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn({
  sessionKey: "agent:my-agent:telegram:direct:12345",
  prompt: "Background task complete. Here are the results...",
  source: "my-plugin",
});
```

### Bundled Extension: `gateway-restart`

A reference implementation that demonstrates the followup turn API:

- **Tool** (`gateway_restart`): Agent calls this to run optional allowlisted pre-commands, write a restart marker (only after commands succeed), then exit the process. Systemd (or equivalent) auto-restarts the gateway.

- **Service** (`gateway-restart-watcher`): On startup, checks for the restart marker. If found, calls `enqueueFollowupTurn()` to resume the original session with a completion message. Marker is deleted only after successful enqueue — preserved for retry on failure.

Disabled by default (`enabledByDefault: false`). Opt-in via `plugins.entries.gateway-restart.enabled: true`.

## Files Changed

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.ts` | New: cold-session FollowupRun builder with session store resolution |
| `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.ts` | New: plugin runtime followup namespace with hot/cold session detection |
| `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` | Add `followup` type to `PluginRuntimeCore` |
| `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` | Wire `createRuntimeFollowup()` |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/index.ts` | New: gateway-restart plugin (tool + watcher service) |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/openclaw.plugin.json` | New: plugin manifest |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/package.json` | New: package metadata |
| `test/helpers/plugins/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` | Add `followup` to mock |
| `**/**.test.ts` | 24 unit tests across 3 test files |

## Bug Fixes (vs #60951)

1. **Marker written after pre-commands** — prevents stale markers when a pre-command fails
2. **Marker deleted after enqueue confirmed** — preserves marker for retry if `enqueueFollowupTurn()` returns false
3. **Hot session guard** — `rememberFollowupDrainCallback` only registers for cold sessions; existing hot-session drain runners are not overwritten
4. **Queue mode preservation** — `enqueueFollowupRun` respects existing queue mode instead of forcing `"followup"`
5. **Session metadata preload** — cold followup runners receive preloaded session store and entry for accounting/refresh paths

## Testing

- 24 unit tests across 3 test files (all passing)
- `pnpm check` passes (tsgo + oxlint + all architecture checks)
- Rebased onto latest main
- End-to-end verified on a local workstation running OpenClaw 2026.4.8 with Gemma 4 26B via llama.cpp

## Use Cases This Enables

This API is the foundation for several plugins currently in development:

- **`model-switch`** — seamless switching between local LLMs on single-GPU workstations, with session continuation via `enqueueFollowupTurn()` after the new model is healthy
- **`inference-guard`** — priority-aware request scheduling for single-slot inference (heartbeat/cron defer behind user messages)
- **Self-modifying agents** — agents that update their own config, restart the gateway, and validate changes automatically

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm check` passes
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/gateway-restart/index.test.ts` — 6 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] Gateway-restart tool writes marker only after pre-commands succeed
- [ ] Watcher service enqueues followup turn and deletes marker on success
- [ ] Watcher preserves marker on enqueue failure

## AI Disclosure

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude Code). Testing level: **fully tested** — 24 unit tests, `pnpm check` green, end-to-end verified on local hardware. The author understands all code in this PR and can explain design decisions.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Summary
- add Swedish (`sv`) as a supported control UI locale
- generate and register `ui/src/i18n/locales/sv.ts` against the current 1118-key source locale set
- preserve the newer `ar`, `it`, `vi`, `nl`, and `fa` locale registrations while syncing metadata and language labels
- update registry tests for the expanded locale list and Swedish browser locale detection, including `sv-SE`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Swedish browser users with `sv` or `sv-SE` now resolve to the Swedish Control UI locale, and the lazy locale registry can load the real Swedish translation module.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `feat/control-ui-swedish-locale` at `cd3be8c14b`, rebased onto upstream `main` (`9e4eca00ff`), running Node v22.22.2 with repo dependencies installed by pnpm.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the actual Control UI registry and Swedish locale module from the checked-out branch and resolved Swedish browser language values without mocks:

```console
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { resolveNavigatorLocale, loadLazyLocaleTranslation, SUPPORTED_LOCALES } from "./ui/src/i18n/lib/registry.ts"; const sv=await loadLazyLocaleTranslation("sv"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ hasSv: SUPPORTED_LOCALES.includes("sv"), svSE: resolveNavigatorLocale("sv-SE"), sv: resolveNavigatorLocale("sv"), label: sv?.languages?.sv, commonHealth: sv?.common?.health }, null, 2));'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real local checkout:

```console
{
  "hasSv": true,
  "svSE": "sv",
  "sv": "sv",
  "label": "Svenska",
  "commonHealth": "Status"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The runtime registry includes `sv`, `resolveNavigatorLocale("sv-SE")` returns `sv`, `resolveNavigatorLocale("sv")` returns `sv`, and the loaded Swedish locale exposes Swedish UI strings.

**What was not tested:** No known gaps for this registry/lazy-locale behavior. Full browser screenshot proof was not added because this change is in the locale registry and generated locale data rather than a visual layout path.

## Validation

target.title: # PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)
target.body:
# PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)

## Summary

This PR introduces a stricter, policy-driven filesystem access layer (**PathGuard**) for OpenClaw agent filesystem tools, including local media (image/pdf) loaders.

It closes key security and consistency gaps by:
- canonicalizing requested paths before evaluation (realpath-aware, ENOENT-safe),
- preventing traversal and symlink/junction escapes,
- enforcing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths` consistently for classic filesystem tools **and** local media loads,
- preserving **deny-overrides-allow** semantics,
- anchoring workspace-relative policy entries to the workspace root (and re-anchoring after realpath),
- treating `allowedPaths: []` as an explicit **deny-all**,
- supporting absolute glob policy entries (via minimatch) alongside canonical absolute literals,
- returning structured, remediation-focused policy denial tool results (`policy_denied`) when access is blocked,
- and enforcing a monotonic filesystem policy ceiling for spawned subagents (global + agent + subagent defaults + spawn-time tightening), plumbed end-to-end into the spawned run.

**Important scope note:**

R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”

Remote URLs (`http(s)://`) and inline `data:` inputs are not treated as filesystem paths and are outside this layer.

---

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Tests

---

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39672
- Related #8719

---

## Problem Statement (What was wrong before)

Before this PR, filesystem tool enforcement had multiple gaps:

1) **Path canonicalization and symlink escape handling were fragmented**
- Traversal protections and “what path are we actually touching?” logic were spread across tools/flows.
- Non-existent paths (ENOENT) were tricky: naive realpath calls fail and can allow later-creation escapes.

2) **Policy matching was inconsistent**
- Absolute vs relative entries could be evaluated in different “path spaces”.
- Glob vs non-glob entries could diverge in behavior, especially when “roots” filtering was used as an approximation.

3) **Media tools could diverge from file tool policy**
- Local file references inside image/pdf flows could reopen outside-workspace reads if not enforced per resolved file.

4) **Sandbox and host flows lacked a unified enforcement story**
- Enforcement needed to be identical regardless of whether the run is sandboxed or host-based.

5) **Spawned subagents did not have a guaranteed filesystem policy ceiling**
- Subagents needed an explicit, monotonic ceiling that could not be loosened at spawn time.

---

## What Changed (Design + Implementation)

### 1) Core PathGuard enforcement (`src/security/path-guard.ts`)

PathGuard implements:

- **Canonical resolution for existing paths** via `fs.realpath`.
- **Nearest-existing-parent resolution** for ENOENT paths (prevents “create later via symlink” bypass).
- **Symlink/junction-aware comparisons** in canonical path space.
- **Deny precedence**: deny rules always override allow rules.
- **Workspace-only boundary**: `workspaceOnly` blocks outside-workspace access.
- **Relative entry anchoring**: workspace-relative policy entries are anchored to the workspace root, and re-anchored after realpath resolution.
- **Empty allowlists**: `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit deny-all.
- **Absolute policy entries**:
  - absolute **literals** are canonicalized before comparison,
  - absolute **globs** are supported (matched against canonical real paths) without attempting to realpath the pattern.
- **Glob detection** via `Minimatch(...).hasMagic()` (brace/extglob supported).

### 2) Filesystem tool integration (read/write/edit/apply_patch-style)

- Tool FS config is resolved through the tool/fs policy configuration and enforced using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`.
- Wrappers normalize args and run PathGuard checks on resolved paths.
- `apply_patch` is explicitly guarded by parsing patch hunks and enforcing FS policy on each patch target path (not just args.path/file_path).

### 3) Structured `policy_denied` tool result (model guidance)

When PathGuard denies access, tools return a structured tool result (instead of only throwing a raw error) that:
- states the restriction is system-enforced and cannot be bypassed,
- describes what the tool attempted to do,
- provides actionable remediation options,
- and explicitly points to config keys that can be changed to grant access.

This improves model behavior: fewer useless retries, more direct user remediation.

### 4) Media tool integration (image/pdf local paths)

Local media loads are now policy-checked on the resolved file path:
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`

Key invariant:
- enforcement is per resolved path using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`, not via root-prefix approximations.

### 5) Sandbox behavior

For local media loads in sandbox mode:
- PathGuard runs in sandbox flows too,
- policy root uses `sandbox.root` when sandboxed,
- otherwise `workspaceDir` is used.

### 6) Subagent filesystem policy ceiling (spawn-time tightening + runtime enforcement)

Goal: spawned subagents must never receive broader filesystem access than the configured ceiling.

Policy sources (monotonic tightening):
- Global: `tools.fs`
- Per-agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- Subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- Sessions-spawn defaults/ceiling: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (applied when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`)
- Spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge semantics:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: enforced as a ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

Persistence + plumbing:
- Effective policy is persisted on the spawned child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) via `sessions.patch`.
- Gateway plumbs it into the child run as ingress field `toolFsPolicy`.
- Gateway session resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve `spawnedToolFsPolicy` (no widening after reset).
- Embedded runner passes it into tool construction.
- `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an `fsPolicy` override and uses it as the effective policy for that run.
- Nested spawns inherit the parent session’s `spawnedToolFsPolicy` ceiling before applying config/tool-call tightening.

---

## Configuration (for contributors)

### Filesystem policy knobs

- `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: boolean`
- `tools.fs.allowedPaths: string[] | undefined` (empty array means deny-all)
- `tools.fs.denyPaths: string[] | undefined`

Subagent ceiling:
- `tools.subagents.fs.{workspaceOnly, allowedPaths, denyPaths}`

Sessions-spawn defaults + spawn-time tightening:
- `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (config default ceiling/tightening)
- `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (tool arg tightening; can only further restrict)

Notes:
- Deny always wins.
- Relative entries are workspace-anchored.
- Policies are evaluated against canonicalized real paths.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals / Out of Scope

- Exec sandboxing is not implemented here.
  R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”
- No shell argument parsing.
- Remote source governance is out-of-scope.

---

## Implementation Notes (for contributors)

### Policy evaluation edge cases covered

- `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit **deny-all** (not “no restriction”).
- Relative literal entries are **re-anchored after realpath**: if a workspace-relative entry canonicalizes outside the workspace (e.g. it points at a symlink to an external dir), it **does not** match outside-workspace targets.
- Absolute glob entries support symlink-alias authoring by **canonicalizing the non-glob prefix** before matching.
  - When rebuilding the rewritten pattern, the implementation **normalizes slash joining** so root-anchored globs stay semantically equivalent (e.g. `"/**/*.pem"` must not become `"//**/*.pem"`).

### Tool behavior on denial (model guidance)

When a filesystem access is blocked by PathGuard, tools return a structured `policy_denied` result (English text + structured fields) so models:
- do not attempt “bypass retries”,
- clearly communicate what was attempted,
- present actionable remediation options,
- and point to config keys (`tools.fs.*`) for operators to change if access is intended.

### Subagent filesystem ceiling (monotonic tightening)

Effective subagent policy is computed as a ceiling and is monotonic (cannot be widened by spawn calls):
- global: `tools.fs`
- agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- sessions-spawn defaults: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`
- spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` / tool arg `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge intent:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

The effective ceiling is persisted on the child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) and forwarded into the embedded runner (`toolFsPolicy` → `createOpenClawCodingTools({ fsPolicy })`).

---

## Verification

- CI checks and tests pass (per latest runs).
- Added/maintained coverage ensuring `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` is applied as a default tightening when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`.
- Verified critical denial scenarios: traversal, symlink/junction escapes, ENOENT parent-walk, deny precedence, empty allowlist deny-all, sandbox media enforcement, apply_patch enforcement, and subagent ceiling propagation.
- Verified monotonic nested spawning: a tightened subagent cannot spawn a child with broader FS access (parent `spawnedToolFsPolicy` is inherited).
- Verified gateway resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve the spawned FS ceiling (no widening after reset).

---

## Reviewer / Contributor Notes (Do not regress these invariants)

- Do not replace per-path enforcement with root-prefix approximations for glob policies.
- Keep deny-overrides-allow.
- Keep relative policy entries anchored to workspace (including after realpath).
- Keep sandbox and host local-file enforcement aligned.
- Keep local filesystem policy and remote-source policy separate concepts.
- Keep subagent policy tightening monotonic (no privilege escalation vs ceiling).
- Remember: exec is outside this enforcement layer.

---

## Rollback / Recovery

- Disable restrictions by setting `workspaceOnly: false` and clearing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths`.
- No data migration required.

target.title: feat(plugins): add optional api.resetSession()
target.body:
## Summary

`main` already has gateway-side session reset support through `performGatewaySessionReset()`.

This PR adds optional plugin-side `api.resetSession(key, reason?)` support on top of the current mainline architecture.

- Adds `PluginResetSessionResult` type and optional `resetSession` on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- Wires `api.resetSession(...)` in the plugin registry only in full registration mode and only when the gateway exposes `sessions.reset`
- Reuses gateway-owned canonicalization and reset resolution (no plugin-side duplicate logic)
- Unifies config snapshot usage in `performGatewaySessionReset` to eliminate config-drift between target resolution and entry lookup
- Adds per-canonical-key in-flight guard in the reset service
- Exports `PluginResetSessionResult` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`

## Scope

This PR:
- adds `PluginResetSessionResult` to `src/plugins/types.ts`
- adds optional `resetSession` to `OpenClawPluginApi`
- adds builder support in `src/plugins/api-builder.ts`
- wires `api.resetSession(...)` in `src/plugins/registry.ts` (full-mode + capability gated)
- adds optional `existingCfg` param to `loadSessionEntry` in `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- adds in-flight guard and unified resolution in `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts`
- exports the result type from `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
- adds focused plugin contract tests (17 tests)
- refreshes generated plugin SDK API baselines

This PR does not:
- change gateway reset RPC behavior
- broaden session architecture scope beyond the plugin API surface
- revive any stale-branch refactor baggage from the closed #43375

## Behavior

`api.resetSession(key, reason?)`:
- accepts `"new"` or `"reset"` reason values (defaults to `"new"`)
- trims and validates the key
- delegates to gateway-owned `performGatewaySessionReset`
- resolves to a structured plugin result instead of rejecting for operational failures
- absent in setup-only, setup-runtime, and cli-metadata registration modes
- absent when gateway does not expose `sessions.reset`

Success: `{ ok: true, key, sessionId }`
Failure: `{ ok: false, key, code, message }`

## Local Verification

```
pnpm test -- src/plugins/reset-session.test.ts     # 17/17 pass
pnpm test -- src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts      # 77/77 pass
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check                           # OK
pnpm tsgo                                           # no new errors
```

Replaces #43375 (closed, stale branch).

target.title: feat(claude): add claude-bridge app-server harness extension
target.body:
## Summary

TL;DR:
Inspiration: https://openclaw.ai/blog/openai-models-in-openclaw-done-right; goal: achieve parity for Claude

New feature; issue https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93793

OpenClaw currently has no first-class harness for Anthropic models. Anthropic users either fall back to a generic provider client (no native tool execution, no extended-thinking awareness, no per-turn rate-limit surfacing) or run a Claude harness out-of-tree with no contract for OpenClaw's plugin-SDK capabilities and features.

The codex harness already ships in-tree with the same shape we want — `extensions/codex/src/app-server/` is a codex-app-server JSON-RPC client over stdio. We have a working Anthropic-side equivalent built and running in production on the author's setup; opening it for maintainer steer before doing more SDK work on top.

Harness: this PR
JSON-RPC server: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge

**What is the intended outcome?**

- Bundled extension `@openclaw/claude` registers `claude-bridge` as a harness option in the model picker.
- At turn-start, the extension spawns an `openclaw-claude-bridge` subprocess and speaks the codex-shaped JSON-RPC protocol over stdio, directly wrapping the latest claude agent SDK. The server (currently published as `@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge`  https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge — fork-preview path; would migrate to `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` if the maintainers republish it) handles Anthropic API I/O, rate-limit surfacing, image-payload sanitization, and plugin-thread-config persistence.
- Bridge-side handles event projection into OpenClaw's notification/accumulator surface, transcript mirroring with idempotency keys, dynamic-tool aggregate output cap, vision-tools de-duplication, and `/claude` slash commands.
- See `extensions/claude/README.md` for the full feature map.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**

- Republishing the server under `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` — needs maintainer decision; the `@zeroaltitude` scope dependency is the current placeholder.
- Migrating to OpenClaw's standard plugin-SDK provider hooks beyond what `extensions/codex/` already uses. The bridge follows codex's pattern verbatim where applicable.
- Anything outside the harness bridge surface.

**What does success look like?** Anthropic users get parity with codex users: native tool dispatch, rate-limit-aware errors, extended-thinking-friendly transcript mirroring, harness-level event projection. No regressions to existing harnesses.

**What should reviewers focus on?**

1. **Scope concern — am I taking too much core?** The branch touches `src/plugins/command-registration.ts`, `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.ts`, `src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-types.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-native-slash-fast-path.ts`, `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts`, and `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`. Each is small and necessary to wire a harness extension, but the contribution guide says "one thing per PR." Happy to split into prep PRs first if maintainers prefer.
2. **External-plugin vs. bundled-extension** — Per CONTRIBUTING.md "most features should be third-party plugins." Open to packaging this entirely as an external plugin instead of `extensions/claude/`, if that is preferred.
3. **Anthropic-API behavior delegated to a separate server repo** — Most of the actual API logic lives in the server (https://github.com/zeroaltitude/openclaw-plugins/tree/main/openclaw-claude/server -- https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge). Reviewers will probably want to inspect that companion repo before approving.
4. **`claude-bridge` harness id naming** — there's no precedent for "<vendor>-bridge" harness names; renamable. (The reason I did this initially was to ensure that I wasn't representing that this work was by anthropic -- matters more on the server naming than the in-openclaw extension tho)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Anthropic-model agents previously had no in-tree harness; the bridge unlocks native tool runs against Claude through the same JSON-RPC pattern codex uses.
- **Real environment tested:** Author's openclaw gateway running on Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu), Node 22.x. Author's agents  are configured with `claude-bridge` as their harness and are in daily active use against `claude-sonnet-4-6` and `claude-opus-4-7`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
  2. `openclaw gateway restart --deep`
  3. Send a Slack DM to one of the claude-bridge agents
  4. Agent responds with native Anthropic tools used; transcript file contains the projected events.
  5. `/claude status` in the same thread prints shared-client liveness.
- **Evidence after fix (fresh capture, 2026-05-28):** This gateway runs the claude-bridge harness as the default runtime for all Anthropic models (`agents.defaults.models.*.agentRuntime.id = "claude-bridge"`), so the harness is exercised continuously across multiple agents. Captured live:
  1. **Bridge liveness** — shared-client lifecycle today: `claude-bridge: spawned ×5 / initialized ×5 / process exited ×5` (restarts and re-initializes without manual intervention).
  2. **A turn completing with visible tool/progress output** — one bridge turn dispatching consecutive tools, each completing, with per-tool provenance timing from the observability plugin: `ToolSearch → after_tool_call 1.9ms`, `exec → 1.7ms`, `Write → 1.9ms`, `Agent → 1.9ms`.
  3. **Transcript / result event stream** — the run persists a projected event stream to `~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/sessions/<uuid>.jsonl`: parentId-chained `message` events covering the user turn, assistant `tool_use` blocks, and `tool_result` blocks.
  4. **`/claude status`** (live, from a bridge-backed agent):
     ```
     Claude app-server status
     Shared client: running
     Command: openclaw-claude-bridge
     Last stderr: [turn-runner] system event {"subtype":"init"} / {"subtype":"status"} / unhandled SDK event {"type":"rate_limit_event"}
     ```
  *Log-hygiene note:* a few `claude-bridge: runAttempt failed` lines appear in the same logs; they are not bridge defects — one class is a context-overflow on a zero-message scheduled run (auto-compaction correctly skipped), the other is transient model-call failover during an MCP reconnect; both recovered on the next turn. Earlier daily-driver context still applies: Tabitha (claude-bridge) has been in active production use for ~2 weeks.
- **Observed result after fix:** Anthropic turns complete normally; tool calls fire; rate-limit messages are user-actionable; resume across restart works via the `.claude-binding.json` sidecar.
- **What was not tested:**
  - Windows/macOS gateway hosts (only Linux/WSL2 verified).
  - Bun runtime (Node 22 only).
  - Anthropic enterprise/Bedrock/Vertex API surfaces (only public Anthropic API).
  - Concurrent multi-account scenarios beyond 3 agents.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Author runs a single-host gateway. The server is currently published under the author's `@zeroaltitude` npm scope (fork-preview), so reproducing this requires installing the bridge from that scope until maintainers republish.
- **Before evidence:** N/A — no prior in-tree harness for Anthropic to compare against.

## Tests and validation

- Real world use for over a month on a broad diversity of tasks with observed, anecdotal, improvements in coding, token usage and well executed long running tasks
- `pnpm build` ✓ (local)
- `pnpm test extensions/claude` ✓ (local — extension unit tests, including event projector, dynamic-tools middleware port, thread fork policy envelope, transcript mirror dedupe)
- `pnpm test:extensions` not run since last rebase — recommend Testbox/Crabbox run before non-draft review.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit per contributor rule; maintainer to add on land.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — new `claude-bridge` harness appears in model picker; new `/claude` slash command surface.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** Yes — new plugin `configSchema` (see `extensions/claude/openclaw.plugin.json`). Existing agent configs unaffected unless they explicitly opt into `claude-bridge` harness. Doctor contract added (`extensions/claude/doctor-contract-api.ts`).
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** Yes (intentional). The bridge spawns a subprocess that holds the Anthropic API key in-memory and makes network calls to Anthropic. Subprocess lifecycle is owned by the harness, not user shell. The plugin SDK enabler changes (e.g. `command-registration.ts`) are additive and follow existing codex-extension precedent.
- **Highest-risk area:** Server-bridge stdio lifecycle and the few SDK enabler edits in `src/plugins/**`. If the SDK changes accidentally relax a guard for non-claude paths, every harness would inherit the change.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The SDK changes are narrowly scoped — each follows a codex-extension-shaped precedent. Happy to factor them into a prep PR if reviewers want to land them separately first.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer steer on scope: (a) accept as bundled extension as-is, (b) split SDK enablers into a prep PR first, or (c) request packaging as an external plugin.
- **Still waiting on:** Maintainer decision on the above. CI run on this push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** None yet — opening draft.

---

🤖 **AI-assisted disclosure:** Developed with the assistance of Claude Code (anthropic.com/claude-code). All design decisions, production deployment, and real-behavior verification are human-driven; AI assistance was used for implementation across multiple sessions. Session transcripts available on request.

target.title: fix(install): manage auth-profile env refs via OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #95895. When a provider API key such as `GEMINI_API_KEY` is configured via an auth profile, `openclaw gateway install` was writing the key as a plaintext literal in the generated systemd/LaunchAgent service file. All other provider secrets were already handled via `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` and resolved at runtime; auth-profile env refs were the missing source.

## Changes

- `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.ts`: include auth-profile env refs when computing `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`, so they are removed from inline service env values and referenced via the managed-keys list instead.
- `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts`: update existing auth-profile assertions and add a GEMINI_API_KEY regression test.
- `scripts/repro/issue-95895-auth-profile-managed-env-keys.mts`: standalone reproduction script that runs `buildGatewayInstallPlan` against a real temp home and verifies the key is managed, not inline.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: auth-profile `GEMINI_API_KEY` no longer appears as a plaintext literal in the generated gateway service environment; it is listed in `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 24.13, source tree at current main.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `pnpm build`
  - `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-95895-auth-profile-managed-env-keys.mts`
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```text
  OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS=GEMINI_API_KEY
  GEMINI_API_KEY inline=(not inline - good)
  PASS: Auth-profile GEMINI_API_KEY is managed, not written as plaintext.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: `plan.environment.GEMINI_API_KEY` is `undefined`; `plan.environment.OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` contains `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
- **What was not tested**: Actual systemd service file rendering on a host with `systemctl`; the fix is at the install-plan environment level.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts --run` — 45 passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts` — passed
- `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-95895-auth-profile-managed-env-keys.mts` — PASS

AI-assisted: yes.

target.title: fix(agents): bootstrap non-main models.json on skip to prevent Unknown model after agents.create
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `agents.create` creates a valid non-main agent (config/workspace), but does not create `models.json` by design. Under a narrow startup path, this can later fail with `Unknown model`.
- Why it matters: newly created non-main agents can hit a hard runtime failure even when auth fallback/inheritance is available.
- What changed: when models planning returns `action: "skip"`, `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` now performs a one-time bootstrap from main agent `models.json` only if target is non-main and target `models.json` is missing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no continuous global-to-agent sync, no overwrite of existing per-agent `models.json`, no change to `agents.create` behavior itself.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #58289
- Related #22747
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `planOpenClawModelsJson` returns `skip` when providers resolve empty; `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` previously returned early (`wrote: false`) without creating/bootstrapping missing non-main `models.json`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no fallback path for missing non-main `models.json` in the `skip` branch.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): auth store already supports main→non-main inheritance/fallback, but model registry bootstrap was asymmetric.
- Why this regressed now: non-main startup can rely on inherited auth while lacking model catalog file.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: not a request for full global sync; this is a narrow runtime-failure prevention path.

- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the exact `skip` branch and validates one-time bootstrap behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none specific to this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (new test added).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- After `agents.create`, a newly created non-main agent that enters the empty-provider `skip` path no longer proceeds with a missing model catalog and `Unknown model` hard failure.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
agents.create -> non-main agent exists (no models.json by design)

After:

```

### Steps

1. Call `agents.create` to create a new non-main agent.
2. Confirm new agent dir exists and `models.json` is initially absent (current expected behavior of create path).
3. Ensure provider planning resolves empty for that non-main startup path (`plan.action === "skip"`).
4. Start/spawn session for that non-main agent.

### Expected

- Evidence notes:

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A non-main agent created through `agents.create` can have a valid agent directory but no `models.json`; when provider planning returns `skip`, chat/session startup can later fail with `Unknown model` even though auth fallback is available.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 packaged OpenClaw Gateway workflow plus a local OpenClaw checkout with Node 22. Local paths, agent names, and provider configuration are redacted.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created the non-main HR agent through `agents.create`, confirmed the target agent `models.json` was absent before startup, started the non-main chat/session path that enters `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, and checked the target agent directory after startup.

before startup: <state-dir>/agents/<redacted-hr-agent>/agent/models.json missing
ensureOpenClawModelsJson: plan.action=skip
ensureOpenClawModelsJson: inherited models.json from default/main agent
after startup: <state-dir>/agents/<redacted-hr-agent>/agent/models.json exists
chat startup: completed without Unknown model
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The non-main agent no longer continues into runtime with a missing model catalog in this narrow skip path; `models.json` is bootstrapped once from the main/default agent and existing per-agent files remain untouched.
- **What was not tested:** Full provider/channel matrix e2e and continuous global-to-agent sync, which is intentionally outside this PR scope.

## Human Verification (required)

- non-main + missing target models + skip branch + existing main models => target created and copied.
- existing per-agent target models remains untouched.
- main agent path does not self-copy.

- Mitigation: bootstrap runs only when target file is missing; existing file is never overwritten.

target.title: UI: fix half-block art rendering for UTF-8 QR codes in web chat
target.body:
## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: UTF-8 QR codes (using half-block characters ▀▄█) render broken in the web chat dashboard — default `
` line-height creates gaps between rows, background/foreground colors don't match the block-character encoding, and the characters don't tile into a scannable image.
- Why it matters: QR codes sent by tools (e.g. Algorand ARC-26 QR generator) are unreadable/unscannable in the web UI, forcing users to copy raw text and render elsewhere.
- What changed: Added `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` detection in `markdown.ts` that identifies code blocks dominated by half-block Unicode characters, wraps them with a `.half-block-art` CSS class, and applies tight line-height (0.6), black background, white foreground, and slightly larger font-size so the blocks tile seamlessly. CSS added for both `.chat-text` (chat messages) and `.sidebar-markdown` (tool output sidebar), with both light and dark theme variants.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the markdown parsing pipeline, sanitization, or any other code block rendering. Only half-block-art detection and styling were added.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Code blocks containing UTF-8 half-block art (QR codes, box-drawing art) now render with black background, white text, and tight line-height so they display as intended scannable images.
- No change to normal code block rendering.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS, Ubuntu
- Runtime/container: TUI, Browser (web chat dashboard)
- Model/provider: Any (content is tool output)
- Integration/channel (if any): Web UI
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Open the web chat dashboard
2. Have a tool or assistant send a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. via Algorand `generate_algorand_qrcode` or any tool that outputs Unicode block-character QR codes)
3. Observe the code block rendering

### Expected

- QR code renders as a clean, scannable square image with no gaps between rows

### Actual

- Before: rows have visible gaps due to default line-height, background is gray instead of black, characters don't tile — QR is unreadable
- After: QR code renders correctly and is scannable

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: UTF-8 QR codes built from half-block characters (▀▄█) rendered broken in the web chat dashboard — the default `
` line-height left gaps between rows and the gray background/foreground did not match the block-character encoding, so the codes were unscannable. This patch detects half-block art, wraps it in a `.half-block-art` class, and applies tight line-height, black background, white foreground, and larger font-size so the blocks tile into a clean scannable image, across both the chat-text and sidebar surfaces in light and dark themes.

Real environment tested: OpenClaw web chat dashboard running locally on macOS and Ubuntu (Node 22), rendering live tool output that emits a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. the Algorand ARC-26 `generate_algorand_qrcode` tool).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Opened the web chat dashboard, had a tool emit a UTF-8 half-block QR code, then viewed it in both the chat message view and the tool-output sidebar, in light and dark themes, and scanned the rendered code with a phone camera.

Evidence after fix:

**Before**



**After**





Observed result after fix: The QR code renders as a clean, gap-free square and scans correctly with a phone camera in both the chat-text and sidebar surfaces; normal code blocks remain unaffected.

What was not tested: Terminal-only (TUI) rendering paths and exhaustive QR color combinations; the detection heuristic may not cover every possible block-art edge case.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: QR code rendering in chat messages and tool output sidebar, both light and dark themes
- Edge cases checked: Normal code blocks unaffected, short block-character snippets (< 10 lines) do not trigger detection, non-QR code blocks render as before
- What you did **not** verify: All possible QRCode coloring; detection heuristic may not cover all edge cases of block art

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the `.half-block-art` CSS rules in `text.css` and `sidebar.css`, and remove the `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` branch in `markdown.ts`
- Files/config to restore: `ui/src/styles/chat/text.css`, `ui/src/styles/chat/sidebar.css`, `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Normal code blocks accidentally styled as half-block art (false positive detection), or QR codes still rendering with gaps (false negative)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: False positive detection on code blocks that happen to contain many block characters but aren't QR/art
  - Mitigation: Detection requires 60%+ of lines to contain half-block characters AND at least 10 lines — unlikely for normal code. Worst case is cosmetic (black bg + tight line-height on a non-QR block).

target.title: fix(agents): hint when unknown tools are skills
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** When a model mistakenly calls a skill name as a tool (e.g. `my-skill`), the error message is a generic "Tool not found" with no guidance, so the model keeps retrying or gives up. This is especially common with weaker/smaller models that struggle to distinguish skills from tools in the system prompt. - **Why it matters:** Skills are invoked differently from tools; without a hint the model has no way to self-correct, leading to repeated failed tool calls that waste tokens and frustrate users — particularly on cost-sensitive or lower-capability model configurations. - **What changed:** Register an `afterToolCall` hook in `attempt.ts` that intercepts error tool results, detects unknown-tool errors matching a registered skill name, and rewrites the message to `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` Also extracted `resolveUnknownToolName` helper in `errors.ts` (was inline regex) and exported `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` for the hook to call. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `formatAssistantErrorText` signature and all its callers are untouched. No new params threaded through `run.ts`, `payloads.ts`, `subscribe.types.ts`, or lifecycle handlers. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Skills / tool execution ## Linked Issue/PR None ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - When a model calls a skill name as a tool and the skill is registered, the error tool result now says: `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` - When the tool name does not match any registered skill, behavior is unchanged (generic "Tool not found"). ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) - Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm - Model/provider: Claude (Anthropic) - Relevant config: workspace with registered skills (e.g. `my-skill`, `another-skill`) ### Steps 1. Start an OpenClaw agent session with skills registered 2. Prompt the model to call a skill name as if it were a tool (e.g. "use tool my-skill") 3. Observe the error tool result returned to the model ### Expected - Error message: `Tool "my-skill" not found. "my-skill" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` ### Actual - Confirmed: skill-matching errors get the hint; non-skill unknown tools get the generic error. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets Manually tested two scenarios: 1. Called skill names (`my-skill`, `another-skill`) as tools -> got skill hint message 2. Called nonexistent name (`banana123`) as tool -> got generic "Tool not found" ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Both skill-match and non-match unknown tool errors in a live OpenClaw agent session. - **Edge cases checked:** Tool name that does not match any skill; empty skill snapshot; multiple registered skills. - **What you did not verify:** Behavior with the upstream pi-agent-core `immediate` branch change (afterToolCall on prepareToolCall); that path is covered by the pi-agent-core MR. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit `e527e3c111`; the `afterToolCall` hook is additive and removing it restores default unknown-tool errors. - Files/config to restore: `errors.ts`, `attempt.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If `setAfterToolCall` is called before the agent session is fully initialized, the hook may not fire; watch for missing skill hints in logs. ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` regex may not match all provider error formats. - **Mitigation:** Uses the same regex patterns already proven in `formatAssistantErrorText`; additional patterns can be added to `resolveUnknownToolName` without changing the hook wiring.

target.title: feat(session): add chat-channel session switching with LRU history retention
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Chat channels did not support listing or switching between prior sessions under the same SessionKey, and follow-up fixes were needed to preserve switchable transcripts until LRU eviction and restore per-session usage/compaction snapshots correctly.
- Why it matters: Without in-chat session switching, users cannot navigate past conversations from Feishu/DingTalk/Telegram/Discord-style chat surfaces; incorrect transcript/archive or snapshot behavior would also make `/session back`, `/sessions`, and `/status`-adjacent state feel unreliable.
- What changed: Added `/sessions`, `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for chat channels; introduced per-SessionKey LRU `sessionHistory`; preserved switchable transcripts until history eviction; restored token/context/compaction snapshots on switch; documented matching/order/retention behavior; cleaned up reset semantics so `session.historyLimit=0` truly disables switchable history.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change SessionKey routing, does not add cross-SessionKey switching, does not add named sessions/UI picker flows, and does not change channel plugin-specific transport behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #9959
- Related #28168

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Added `/sessions` to list switchable sessions for the current chat SessionKey.
- Added `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for in-chat session switching.
- Current session is included in `/sessions`, marked as `[current]`.
- `/sessions` now shows compact single-line preview rows with session ID prefix and truncated preview text.
- Session switching now restores stored token/context/compaction snapshots for the target session.
- `session.historyLimit` now consistently supports `0` as “disable switchable session history”.
- Transcripts for sessions still in history remain switchable; transcripts are archived when evicted from the LRU history queue.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw dev runtime / source tree
- Model/provider: N/A for core session-switch logic
- Integration/channel (if any): chat-channel command flow; manually validated in a real chat session
- Relevant config (redacted):
  - `session.historyLimit: 5` (default path)
  - `session.historyLimit: 0` (disable-history regression path)

### Steps

1. Start a chat conversation and create multiple sessions with `/new`.
2. Run `/sessions`, then switch via `/session <number>` or `/session back`.
3. Continue creating sessions until the history limit is exceeded.

### Expected

- `/sessions` lists current + recent switchable sessions in LRU order.
- Switching restores the target session’s stored metadata/snapshots.
- Sessions still present in history remain switchable.
- The oldest session is archived only after LRU eviction.
- With `session.historyLimit=0`, switchable history is disabled and the previous session is archived immediately on reset.

### Actual

- Behavior matches the expected results above in targeted tests and manual validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `/sessions` output in chat
  - `/session back` restores the previous session
  - switching to a still-retained historical session restores expected context/state
  - LRU eviction archives only the oldest evicted session
  - daily reset path preserves switchable history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` disables switchable history and archives immediately on reset
- Edge cases checked:
  - history boundary behavior at the configured limit
  - selecting current session is a no-op
  - unique-prefix SessionId selection behavior
  - stale/daily-reset session rollover
- What you did **not** verify:
  - every individual chat provider/plugin surface
  - cross-SessionKey switching (out of scope)
  - any UI/card-based session picker (out of scope)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - revert this PR, or set `session.historyLimit=0` to disable switchable session history
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`
  - `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
  - `src/config/types.base.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `/sessions` shows stale/incorrect ordering
  - switched sessions lose expected token/context/compaction snapshot state
  - transcripts are archived too early while still present in history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` does not disable switchable history

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: LRU eviction/archive timing could regress and archive a session that should still remain switchable.
  - Mitigation: Added/updated targeted tests covering retained-history vs evicted-history behavior.

- Risk: Snapshot restore could leave token/context/compaction state inconsistent after switching.
  - Mitigation: Added lifecycle tests covering restore and missing-metadata fallback behavior.

- Risk: Docs could drift from implementation again as follow-up fixes land.
  - Mitigation: Updated `docs/design/chat-session-switch.md` to match the current implementation semantics in this PR.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: Yes

I used an AI assistant for code review, doc cleanup, and targeted test updates. I manually reviewed the final diff, understood the changes, and verified the session-switch flows myself.

target.title: feat(control-ui): render Mermaid code fences in webchat
target.body:
## Summary
- Render fenced ` ```mermaid ` blocks in Control UI webchat with lazy-loaded Mermaid and dedicated chat styling.
- Keep Mermaid source blocks collapsible and copy-friendly in the transcript UI.
- Harden Mermaid rendering:
  - case-insensitive language fence detection (`mermaid` / `Mermaid`)
  - sanitize rendered SVG before insertion
  - guard against transient module-load/render failures

## Screenshots
Rendered Mermaid diagram in Control UI webchat:

![Mermaid diagram rendered in Control UI webchat](https://files.catbox.moe/efp3l7.png)

## CI/compat follow-ups included in this PR
- Stabilize channel contract tests by moving some imports from module scope into test scope to avoid eager side effects.
- Update contract registry imports to use explicit extension internals where runtime APIs no longer expose these helpers.
- Make bundle MCP path assertions robust across symlink/realpath environments (Windows/CI path normalization).

## Files touched
- UI Mermaid integration:
  - `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/markdown.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/mermaid.ts`
  - `ui/src/styles/chat.css`
  - `ui/src/styles/chat/mermaid.css`
