target.title: feat(android): add Wear OS app MVP
target.body: ## Summary > This is the follow up PR of #46279 > It was heavily written with the help of Gemini 3.1 Pro (mainly for building UI) and Codex GPT 5.4, including lots of review iterations. > During the implementation of the previous PR I moved more and more logic into a shared layer, which caused lots of review comments about code I didn't write. I tried to let Codex write regression tests for several of these cases to ensure that I don't break features. > Manual testing with my Pixel Watch 2 and Pixel 8 Pro was part of the whole process. Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: OpenClaw did not have a Wear OS companion. - Why it matters: The watch needs a compact, reliable chat surface with fast reply actions, honest connection state, and behavior that still works on tiny round screens and when the app is backgrounded. - What changed: Added a new Wear OS app module, phone-side Wear proxy service for Tailscale support, direct watch gateway client, transcript-first chat UI, session picker, split settings screens, tile + complication support, background reply notifications with quick reply. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change gateway orchestration semantics, tools/skills behavior, desktop/iOS/macOS clients, or the Android phone app's broader UI theme beyond the phone-side Wear proxy integration. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # (none) - Related # (none) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - New Wear OS companion app with a transcript-first chat flow and a two-page swipe layout. - Chat opens on the transcript page and follows the latest message by default; the primary edge action switches between `Reply` and `Latest`. - Added session picker, chat settings, connection settings, reconnect flow, and direct-vs-phone-proxy connection support on the watch. - Added Wear tile and complication entry points. - Added native watch notifications for background replies, including quick reply via text/voice dictation. - Added optional TTS playback and configurable screen-awake behavior on the watch. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The Wear app adds `POST_NOTIFICATIONS`, Wear Data Layer messaging, direct gateway WebSocket access, and storage of watch-side gateway config including token-based direct auth. Risk is constrained by using the existing authenticated gateway paths, limiting watch data access to the same session/gateway calls already exposed by the phone client, honoring platform notification permission, and keeping watch auth/config local to the Wear app. Phone-proxy traffic stays within the paired phone/watch channel and only proxies requests through the phone's active operator session. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Android phone + Wear OS 3/4 watches; manually tested on Pixel Watch 2 and Wear round emulators (small + large) - Runtime/container: Android Studio/Gradle debug builds - Model/provider: Existing OpenClaw gateway via phone proxy and direct gateway modes - Integration/channel (if any): Wear Data Layer between paired phone and watch - Relevant config (redacted): Matching debug-signed phone + watch builds; direct mode host/port/token/TLS when used; phone-proxy mode with phone app installed and signed the same way ### Steps 1. Build and install matching debug phone and Wear builds, then launch the Wear app on a paired watch. 2. Verify transcript flow, reply actions, session switching, settings, direct mode, phone-proxy mode, tile/complication entry points, and reopen/background behavior. 3. Send assistant replies while the watch app is foregrounded and backgrounded, then verify reply notifications, quick reply, reconnect/disconnect behavior, and latest-message scrolling. ### Expected - The watch should show a compact, reliable chat UX with correct connection state, preserved transcript state, working reply paths, and background reply notifications. ### Actual - Matches expected in manual testing on Pixel Watch 2 and both small/large Wear emulators. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Actual screenshots from Pixel Watch 2: | | | | |---|---|---| | Empty tanscript view | Empty transcript view | Swipe over to menu | | Scroll down in menu | Scroll further down | Click behavior settings button | | Scroll down | Click screen awake setting | Click screen awake setting again | | Go back and enter connection settings | Scroll down to helper text | Disable phone proxy | | Scroll down to gateway config | Scroll further down | Go back to transcript view | | Scroll down to see reply edge button (*default view* if you open the app≈) | Reply button toggles voice input (or let's you pick first, if disabled in settings) | Thinking animation | | When closing app, notification is delivered| Tile adding dialog with preview of tile | Actual tile | | Watch face with complication at top and bottom right (different formats) | ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Manually tested on a Pixel Watch 2 via Tailscale phone proxy and in both small and large round Wear emulators. Verified chat launch, latest-message behavior, reply flow, session picker, chat settings, connection settings, direct gateway mode, phone-proxy mode, tile launch, complication launch, background reply notifications, and quick reply from the notification. - Edge cases checked: Reopening the app after backgrounding, disconnect/reconnect flow, switching away from and back to the transcript page, scrolling away from latest and jumping back, and testing on both small and large round watch layouts. - What you did **not** verify: every possible Android OEM pairing/background-policy variant, and exhaustive real-network failure cases beyond the implemented reconnect/error handling paths. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: None. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the Wear branch commits or ship only the phone build without installing the Wear module; on-device, switch the watch back to phone-proxy or disconnect direct mode if direct gateway behavior is problematic. - Files/config to restore: `apps/android/wear/**`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/wear/WearProxyService.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/NodeRuntime.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml`, and `apps/android/app/build.gradle.kts`. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: watch stuck showing connected when the proxy/direct gateway is not actually usable, sent messages disappearing after history refresh, explicit disconnect reconnecting by itself, reply notification not surfacing or lacking quick reply, and transcript reopening at the wrong position. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Phone-proxy mode depends on both Wear Data Layer reachability and the phone app having a live operator session. - Mitigation: The watch exposes direct mode as an alternative, surfaces connection state in the UI, and includes reconnect/disconnect controls. - Risk: The second page is dense on the smallest round watches, especially with long session names/keys. - Mitigation: Manual testing covered both small and large round emulator sizes and a real Pixel Watch 2. - Risk: Background reply notifications depend on platform permission and Wear notification presentation. - Mitigation: The app requests notification permission explicitly, and manual verification covered notification delivery plus quick reply behavior.

target.title: Fix dashboard chat run recovery across reconnects
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes two closely related Gateway Dashboard chat reliability problems:

1. Dashboard chat could lose busy-state on reconnect/tab return and incorrectly fall back to `New session` / `Send` while the session still had an active run.
2. Gateway restarts could interrupt an in-flight chat run without leaving a visible assistant-side notice in the chat transcript.

## What changed

- include `activeChatRuns` in the gateway hello snapshot
- recover `chatRunId` / busy state from snapshot on reconnect instead of blindly clearing it
- keep the composer in busy mode while a run is still abortable
- stop forcing sidebar chat navigation back to `main`; prefer the last active chat session
- abort active chat runs during gateway shutdown and inject a durable assistant transcript notice
- process restart sentinel wake immediately on startup and inject a transcript notice for internal sessions without external delivery routes

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-sentinel.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144 pnpm -s tsc --noEmit --pretty false`

## Related

- Closes #41064
- Related: #38836
- Related: #21641
- Related: #26986

target.title: feat(ui): inline rename in the in-chat session picker
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The in-chat session picker only lets users *switch* sessions. Renaming still requires the admin "Sessions" tab, which is cumbersome when juggling several parallel, topic-specific chats — exactly the friction reported in #54397.
- **Change:** Add a per-row inline rename affordance to the chat session-picker popover. An edit button on each row opens an inline input seeded with the current label; Enter / Save commits, Escape / Cancel discards.
- **Reuse, no new write path:** Commit goes through the existing self-refreshing `patchSession(state, key, {label})` flow (the same `sessions.patch` + `loadSessions()` path the admin Sessions tab already uses). Empty/whitespace input clears the label via `normalizeOptionalString`, matching admin-tab semantics exactly.
- **Out of scope (intentional):** chat slash-commands for naming (`/name`, `/new --name`, `/sessions`), switching sessions from text channels, and any LLM auto-suggest. Those are planned as separate, smaller PRs.
- **Success:** Users can rename a session without leaving the chat; behavior and validation are identical to the existing admin rename.

## Linked context

Related #54397, #85502

This is the small "header/sidebar shortcut" that maintainers explicitly invited as a separate polish request when closing #85502 (the admin-tab rename shipped there). It also advances the chat-native session-management UX discussed in #54397. I deliberately did not put `Closes #` because neither issue is a 1:1 match — happy to add one if a maintainer prefers.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** Rename a session inline from the in-chat session picker (open picker → edit icon on a row → type a new label → Enter/Save), including clear-on-empty and Cancel/Escape.
- **Real environment tested:** A live self-hosted OpenClaw gateway (v2026.5.27, Node v22, on WSL2/Ubuntu-24.04) serving this PR branch's built `control-ui`, driven through a real Chromium browser via Playwright. The browser authenticates against the running gateway over the actual WebSocket control channel and exercises the production render path end to end. Endpoint, tokens, and other session labels are redacted.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - Built this branch's `control-ui` and pointed the running gateway at it; confirmed it was live with `curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:<port>/healthz` → `200`.
  - Drove the real UI with a Playwright script (`node rename_e2e.js`, headless Chromium): connect to the gateway → open the in-chat session picker → click a row's inline edit icon → type a new label → press Enter to commit → re-read the session list to confirm persistence → restore the original label.
- **Evidence after fix (browser console + control-channel capture):**

  ```
  $ curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:<port>/healthz   ->   200
  $ node rename_e2e.js   (headless Chromium driving the live gateway)
  [control-ui] rpc method=connect           ok=true   auth=password
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.subscribe ok=true
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.list     ok=true
  picker opened -> inline edit icon present on each session row
  entered inline edit mode -> typed a new label -> pressed Enter
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.patch    ok=true   durationMs=69
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.list     ok=true   (self-refresh)
  verified: the new label is rendered in the picker row AND in the
           left sidebar "Recent" list, with no page reload
  cleanup: original label restored -> sessions.patch ok=true
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** Clicking a row's edit icon enters inline edit mode seeded with the current label. Pressing Enter commits through the existing `sessions.patch` flow (`ok=true`) and the self-refreshing `sessions.list` immediately shows the new name in both the picker row and the sidebar "Recent" list — no page reload and without opening the admin Sessions tab. Cancel/Escape and closing the picker mid-edit discard the draft and leave the label unchanged. The new name persisted across a fresh `sessions.list` read; the original label was then restored.
- **What was not tested:** Non-web channels (this is a Control UI surface only), and exact visual pixel styling across physical devices/browsers other than headless Chromium. The commit path is the same `patchSession` flow the admin Sessions tab already uses, which is additionally covered by the DOM-level regression tests in `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`.

## Tests and validation

- Added 4 focused tests in `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` under "chat session controls": commit via Enter, clear-on-whitespace via Save, cancel via Escape, and discard-on-close (regression guard so closing the picker mid-edit cannot leak a stale rename/draft).
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`, `pnpm ui:i18n:check`, oxlint and oxfmt all green on the changes.

## Risk checklist

- **User-visible behavior change?** Yes — a new rename control in the chat session picker.
- **Config/env/migration change?** No.
- **Security/auth/secrets/network/tool-exec change?** No new write path or permission surface; reuses the existing `sessions.patch` flow already used by the admin rename.
- **Highest-risk area:** ARIA structure — rows are now wrapped in a `<div>` to host the rename button. Mitigation: wrappers are marked `role="presentation"` so options remain owned by the `listbox`. Note: this picker was already a loose listbox (button options, no roving tabindex); a deeper `list`/`listitem` refactor is intentionally left out of scope.
- **Mitigation:** Label normalization/clear semantics are delegated to the same helper as the admin tab; commit uses the existing self-refreshing controller; rename state is cleared on every picker-close path (covered by a test).

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer review of the UX placement and the ARIA `role="presentation"` approach.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** N/A (initial submission).

target.title: test: prefer shared temp dir helpers in auto-reply and install-fallback tests
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Gap finding: tempdir-8h9i0j1k / tempdir-9i0j1k2l

~100+ test files still use raw fs.mkdtemp/fs.mkdtempSync instead of the shared temp directory helpers. This causes:

1. **Disk leaks** — temp dirs created but never cleaned up, accumulating under /tmp/openclaw-*
2. **Parallel test conflicts** — concurrent tests may collide on /tmp paths
3. **Inconsistent patterns** — the project has standardized on shared helpers

## Background

Part of long-running migration: origin 88b87d893d / #87298 established `createSuiteTempRootTracker()` as the standard test temp dir helper. ~50 test files already migrated; ~100+ remain.

## Why This Change Was Made

Migrates first batch of high-risk test files with zero cleanup:

- install-fallback.test.ts: variable overwrite risk — single variable loses earlier dirs
- session-hooks-context.test.ts: mkdtemp called per test case, never cleaned
- abort.test.ts: createAbortConfig leaks root dir
- inbound.test.ts: two test cases without cleanup

## User Impact

Test-only. Reduces /tmp pressure, cleaner CI.

## Evidence

All 4 modified test files + tooling config routing pass.

## CI Fix

`checks-node-compact-small-whole-2` was failing due to a stale test expectation in `test-projects.test.ts`. On latest `main`, `test/scripts/check-workflows.test.ts` imports from `test/helpers/temp-dir.ts`, so the routing test needed to include it. Fixed by adding the entry to the expected routing list.

## Files Changed

| File | Fix |
|------|-----|
| src/skills/lifecycle/install-fallback.test.ts | createSuiteTempRootTracker() |
| src/auto-reply/reply/session-hooks-context.test.ts | createSuiteTempRootTracker() |
| src/auto-reply/reply/abort.test.ts | createSuiteTempRootTracker() |
| src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts | createSuiteTempRootTracker() |
| src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts | update temp-dir routing expectation |

target.title: feat(gateway): surface built-in tool calls as function_call output items on /v1/responses
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds opt-in `gateway.http.endpoints.responses.exposeBuiltInToolCalls` flag.
- When enabled, `/v1/responses` appends a `function_call` output item for every built-in tool the agent invokes (`bash`, `edit`, `find`, `grep`, `ls`, `read`, `write`, `apply_patch`, plus bundled plugin tools), in both JSON and SSE responses, in arrival order at `output_index` 1+.
- Default off; no PI runtime, plugin SDK, or client-tool path changes.

Closes #75074. The issue has the user stories (eval pipelines, audit/replay, "what did the agent do?" UIs) and the before/after JSON.

## Note on the parallel PR #75107

@mrinalgaur2005 opened #75107 ~2 hours after this one with the same approach, and credited me in their CHANGELOG. Their version made several real improvements over my original — I've folded them in here in commit `75bed22698` so the two PRs are at quality parity. Specifically: a dedupe regression test that exercises the client-tool-name filter (which I had implemented but not tested), `status: "in_progress"` on streaming `output_item.added`, dropping events with missing `toolCallId` instead of synthesizing one, expanded JSDoc, `ReadonlySet` typing, and a structured semantics bullet list in the docs. Both authors are credited in the CHANGELOG.

## Why a refactor was in this PR before the polish commit

The first commit on this branch opened a parallel `onAgentEvent` subscription just for tool capture. It worked but was ugly: two listeners per stream request, non-stream and stream output orders diverged. The second commit folds the capture into the streaming path's existing main listener and aligns both paths on the same output order (assistant at index 0, audit items 1+).

## Implementation

- `src/config/{types.gateway,zod-schema,schema.base.generated}.ts`: adds `exposeBuiltInToolCalls?: boolean` to `GatewayHttpResponsesConfig`. The generated JSON-schema baseline is regenerated and committed.
- `src/gateway/openresponses-http.ts`:
  - Pure helper `tryCaptureBuiltInToolCall({ evt, runId, clientToolNames })` returns a `CapturedBuiltInToolCall` or `null` for one event. No internal state, no listeners, no dispose. Drops events from a different run, non-tool streams, non-start phases, names that match a caller-provided client tool, and events with empty/missing `toolCallId`.
  - Streaming path: tool capture is an additional branch in the existing `unsubscribe = onAgentEvent(...)` listener. One subscription per request. Captures are pushed into `streamBuiltInItems` and emitted via `response.output_item.added` (`status: "in_progress"`) / `done` (`status: "completed"`) immediately, with a `nextStreamOutputIndex` counter so audit items, the optional client-tool `function_call`, and the assistant `message` never collide on `output_index`.
  - Non-streaming path: small inline subscription accumulates into a local array. Items are appended after the assistant message.
- `src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts`: four new e2e tests — default-off does not surface, JSON output sequence is asserted as `[message, function_call, function_call]` with correct args, SSE emits `response.output_item.added` for every capture and the final `response.completed` includes them, and the dedupe test for client-tool-name conflicts.
- `docs/gateway/openresponses-http-api.md`: documents the flag with a JSON5 config example and a structured semantics list.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: entry under Unreleased / Changes, links #75074, credits both contributors.

The PI runtime, the `clientTools` admission path, the `pendingToolCalls` delegate path, and `PI_RESERVED_TOOL_NAMES` are all untouched.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts` — 19/19 passing (incl. 4 new tests; existing tests unchanged).
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core` — clean.
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` — clean (baseline regenerated and committed).
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` — clean on touched files.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxlint` (core profile) — 0 warnings, 0 errors on touched files.
- [x] Rebased onto current `origin/main`; `mergeable: MERGEABLE`.

## Out of scope (deliberate)

- Tool result capture — only `phase: "start"` is surfaced. Result capture is a different design conversation (size, redaction, schema-per-tool).
- Streaming token-level deltas of tool args.
- "Delegate mode" where built-ins become caller-executable. That lifts `PI_RESERVED_TOOL_NAMES` and rewires tool admission; it deserves its own issue.

target.title: feat(browser): add agent download actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the agent-facing `browser` tool did not expose the managed browser download helpers even though the CLI/control API already supported `/download` and `/wait/download`.
- Why it matters: agents could inspect pages and snapshots but could not ask the managed browser to click a download ref and return the saved file path.
- What changed: add `download` and `waitfordownload` actions to the browser tool schema/routing, export the existing runtime helpers, document the actions, and add regression coverage for host and node proxy routing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): existing-session download interception remains managed-only; this does not bypass the browser server's existing 501/unsupported behavior for profiles that cannot intercept downloads.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the browser extension already had managed download endpoints and client helpers, but the agent tool schema and routing never exposed `download` / `waitfordownload`. During implementation, the new action routing also needed to align outer transport timeouts with browser-side download waits instead of keeping the older fixed 20s HTTP/proxy timeout.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no agent-tool regression test asserting download action routing or transport timeout propagation.
- Contributing context (if known): prior PR #12378 attempted a similar action surface but did not land.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Scenario the test should lock in: agent `browser` actions route `download` and `waitfordownload` through the host client or node proxy, preserve request bodies, require explicit `ref`/`path` where needed, and give long download waits enough outer transport timeout.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the browser-side download implementation already exists; the risk here is the agent tool contract and proxy/client timeout handoff.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agents can now call the built-in `browser` tool with `action="download"` to click a snapshot ref and save the resulting file, or `action="waitfordownload"` to wait for the next managed download. Results include the saved download path returned by the existing browser control API.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent browser tool] -> [snapshot/action helpers only] -> [no download action]

After:
[agent browser tool] -> [download/waitfordownload] -> [managed browser endpoint] -> [saved file path]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this exposes existing managed browser download endpoints through the agent tool. It keeps the existing browser server/profile capability checks, keeps existing-session download interception managed-only, and constrains saved paths to the browser download path handling already used by the control API.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.8.0-55-generic x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.13.0, pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Browser plugin / built-in browser tool

### Steps

2. Execute `action="download"` with a snapshot `ref` and output `path`, or execute `action="waitfordownload"` with an optional output `path`.
3. For node targets, route the request through `browser.proxy` and preserve both body timeout and outer transport timeout.

### Expected

- The action reaches `/download` or `/wait/download` and returns the browser download payload, including the saved file path.
- Explicit and default download waits are not cut short by the outer host/proxy transport timeout.

target.title: feat(huggingface): add text-to-image generation via hf-inference Inference Providers route
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #63487 in a much smaller, focused form per @steipete's review feedback. Adds **text-to-image only** to the existing `huggingface` plugin, routed through the official Hugging Face Inference Providers `hf-inference` endpoint. No new credentials, no parallel HTTP stack, no out-of-scope capabilities.

Closes / supersedes #63487.

## What's in scope

- New `ImageGenerationProvider` registered by the existing `huggingface` plugin
- Endpoint: `POST https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference/models/<repo_id>` (returns raw image bytes)
- Default model: `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev`; recommended list also exposes FLUX.1-dev / FLUX.1-schnell / Qwen-Image / Hyper-SD (router accepts any compatible repo id)
- Reuses the existing `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN` / `HF_TOKEN` credential — no new auth choice or env var
- `--size <W>x<H>` is forwarded as `parameters.width` / `parameters.height` per the [text-to-image task spec](https://huggingface.co/docs/inference-providers/tasks/text-to-image)
- JPEG output is surfaced when the upstream model emits it (mime + file extension inferred from `Content-Type`)

## Addressing the previous review

All five blockers from #63487 are handled:

1. **`isConfigured` uses the SDK** — `isProviderApiKeyConfigured({ provider: \"huggingface\", agentDir })`, no manual env scanning
2. **HTTP goes through guarded helpers** — `resolveProviderHttpRequestConfig` + `postJsonRequest` + `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`, so SSRF / proxy / timeout / dispatcher policy all behave like every other provider
3. **No parallel auth path** — request-time resolution via `resolveApiKeyForProvider`, same as chat
4. **Plugin manifest declares the contract** — `contracts.imageGenerationProviders: [\"huggingface\"]` in `openclaw.plugin.json`
5. **Tests + docs + changelog** — colocated Vitest spec, docs section under `docs/providers/huggingface.md`, changelog entry under the Unreleased section with `Thanks @lyfuci`

## Out of scope (deliberate)

Per maintainer guidance, this PR is intentionally tight. The following are **not** included and will be separate PRs if/when there's interest:

- Image-to-image / edit
- Multi-image batches
- Other Inference Providers routes (fal-ai, Replicate, Together, Nscale, WaveSpeedAI)
- Video / embeddings / STT

## Test plan

- [x] \`pnpm check:changed\` — 6 lanes green (conflict markers, typecheck extensions, typecheck extension tests, lint extensions, runtime import cycles, tests changed)
- [x] 7 new Vitest cases pass (default model, custom model + size, JPEG mime inference, missing key, upstream HTTP error, empty payload, capability surface)
- [x] Format check clean on the 6 touched files
- [x] End-to-end smoke test against the real \`hf-inference\` endpoint:
  \`\`\`bash
  openclaw infer image generate \\
    --model huggingface/black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell \\
    --prompt \"a serene lake surrounded by mountains at sunset, photorealistic\" \\
    --size 1024x1024 \\
    --output lake.png
  # → 1024x1024 PNG, 800 KB, file lake.png: PNG image data, 1024 x 1024, 8-bit/color RGB
  \`\`\`

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The provider's default model `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev` is no longer served by the `hf-inference` provider (HF routes it to `fal-ai`/`replicate`), so it returned `400 Model not supported by provider hf-inference`; also `count > 1` was silently ignored, and `ssrfPolicy` was not forwarded to the HTTP layer. This proof covers the fixed default (`FLUX.1-schnell`), the `count > 1` rejection, and that the live request path reaches `hf-inference` authenticated.

**Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw checkout built with `pnpm build` (branch head `24a516d8`), Node 24, running the compiled provider from `dist/` against the live `https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference` router with a real `HF_TOKEN`. Not a unit test / mock — the built provider and real HTTP path.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the built provider chunk from `dist/` and called `provider.generateImage(...)` directly — once with `count: 2` (expect rejection) and once with the default model and a real prompt (expect a live HTTP round-trip to hf-inference).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim console output from the built provider:
```
[proof] provider.id = huggingface | defaultModel = black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell
[proof] count:2 -> Hugging Face image generation supports only a single image per request (count must be 1)
[proof2] generateImage error -> Hugging Face image generation failed (HTTP 402): You have depleted your monthly included credits. Purchase pre-paid credits to continue using Inference Providers. [request_id=Root=1-6a3506bf-...]
```

**Observed result after fix:** (1) The shipped build defaults to `FLUX.1-schnell` and rejects `count: 2` with the explicit single-image error. (2) The live `generateImage` → `postJsonRequest` → HF router call returns **HTTP 402** (not 400/401) — proving the request was correctly routed to `hf-inference`, authenticated with the token, and accepted the `FLUX.1-schnell` model; it failed only because this account's monthly image credits are exhausted. This exercises the real SSRF-guarded `postJsonRequest` path and `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`. Default-model change verified live against the HF provider-mapping API (2026-06-19): of the previously-listed repos, only `FLUX.1-schnell` is on `hf-inference`.

**What was not tested:** A successful end-to-end image (saved PNG) could not be produced because the HF account's monthly image credits are exhausted (HTTP 402) — this is an account billing limit, not a code defect; a clean PNG can be generated once credits reset. Requesting maintainer `proof: override` for the generation step, since the routing/auth/model/validation path is proven above.

target.title: feat #94529: auto-derive session title in sidebar
target.body:
## Summary

Control UI session lists now request derived titles from the gateway and fall back to them when a session has no user-set label or display name. This makes the sidebar and chat session picker distinguishable after opening multiple sessions.

## Root Cause

`sessions.list` already supports `includeDerivedTitles`, but the Control UI frontend never requested it, so the sidebar showed generic labels such as "New Session" for fresh sessions.

## Changes

- `ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.ts`: include `includeDerivedTitles: true` in sidebar session list requests.
- `ui/src/ui/chat/session-controls.ts`: include `includeDerivedTitles: true` in chat session picker requests.
- `ui/src/ui/types.ts`: add `derivedTitle?: string` to `GatewaySessionRow`.
- `ui/src/ui/session-display.ts`: use `derivedTitle` as a fallback after explicit `label` and `displayName`.

## Regression Test Plan

- Updated `ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts` to assert `includeDerivedTitles: true` in expected `sessions.list` params.
- Updated `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` for chat session picker params.
- Added `resolveSessionDisplayName` tests in `ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts` covering `derivedTitle` fallback and precedence over explicit labels.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Control UI sidebar and chat session picker showed generic "New Session" labels for fresh sessions instead of derived titles.

Real environment tested: Linux development checkout running the Control UI test harness.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
pnpm test ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts --run
```

Evidence after fix:
```
✓ ui/ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts (78 tests)
✓ ui/ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts (77 tests)
✓ unit-ui/ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts (110 tests)

Test Files 3 passed (3)
Tests 265 passed (265)
```

These tests verify that the frontend now requests `includeDerivedTitles: true` and that `derivedTitle` is used as a fallback display name when no explicit label or displayName is set.

Observed result after fix: `sessions.list` calls from the sidebar and chat session picker include `includeDerivedTitles: true`; `resolveSessionDisplayName` returns the derived title when no explicit label/displayName exists.

What was not tested: A live browser screenshot of the Control UI sidebar rendering multiple sessions with distinct derived titles (requires a running gateway and browser). Requesting maintainer `proof:` override for the missing live UI screenshot.

Closes #94529.

target.title: Fix legacy LLM timeout diagnostics
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve the numeric `agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds` value in `doctor --fix` output instead of silently dropping it.
- Add a one-shot config-load warning when `agents.defaults.llm` is still present, pointing users to `models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds`.
- Cover doctor migration, partial-validation migration, and config-load diagnostics with focused tests.

Fixes #74910

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Legacy `agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds` was removed by `doctor --fix` with only a generic message, so users lost the old timeout value and had no actionable provider-specific migration hint.

**Real environment tested:** Local macOS OpenClaw checkout at `82e9c03`, run with isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` and `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. The temp config contained `agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds: 180` and `models.providers.mlx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```sh
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp>/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state pnpm openclaw health
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp>/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state pnpm openclaw doctor --fix
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the real CLI run:
```text
$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp>/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state pnpm openclaw health
Config invalid
Problem:
  - agents.defaults: Unrecognized key: "llm"
Legacy config keys detected:
  - agents.defaults.llm: agents.defaults.llm is legacy; use models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds for slow model/provider timeouts. Run "openclaw doctor --fix".

Run: openclaw doctor --fix

$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp>/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state pnpm openclaw doctor --fix
Legacy config keys detected
  - agents.defaults.llm: agents.defaults.llm is legacy; use models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds for slow model/provider timeouts. Run "openclaw doctor --fix".

Doctor changes
  Removed agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds: 180; to preserve this behavior, set models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds: 180 for slow providers. Configured providers: mlx.
```

**Observed result after fix:** The real CLI now preserves the old timeout value in the doctor migration output and gives the user the exact provider-level replacement value to configure: `models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds: 180`. It also shows the configured provider hint: `Configured providers: mlx.`

**What was not tested:** No known gaps for this diagnostic-only change; no live LLM request was needed because the fix changes config migration and diagnostics only.

## Tests
- `pnpm test src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.validation.test.ts src/config/io.compat.test.ts`

target.title: feat(openai): add queryTask/documentTask config for Jina v5 task-specific embeddings
target.body:
## Summary

Add typed `queryTask` and `documentTask` configuration under `agents.*.memorySearch` for Jina v5 task-specific embeddings through the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider.

Closes #74419

## Background

Jina Embeddings v5 supports task-specific LoRA adapters via a `task` request-body parameter. Without this config, OpenClaw can call the model but cannot select query/document-optimized adapters such as `retrieval.query` and `retrieval.passage`.

## Changes

- Add `queryTask` and `documentTask` to memory search config, zod schema, resolved config, plugin/SDK embedding option types, labels/help text, and generated config baseline hash.
- Forward `queryTask` on inline query embeddings and `documentTask` on document/batch embedding requests in the OpenAI embedding provider.
- Include `documentTask` in OpenAI batch embedding cache identity.
- Document the Jina v5 task parameters in the OpenAI provider docs and memory config reference.
- Add targeted tests for config propagation, OpenAI request bodies, batch request bodies, and schema help coverage.

## Supported task values

- `retrieval.query` / `retrieval.passage` — asymmetric retrieval
- `text-matching` — symmetric semantic matching
- `clustering` — cluster-friendly embeddings
- `classification` — classification-friendly embeddings
- `separation` — separation-focused embeddings

When left unset, Jina v5 defaults to general-purpose embeddings.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw should emit Jina v5 `task` request-body parameters from `queryTask` for query embeddings and `documentTask` for document/indexing embeddings when using the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux/Node 22, running the actual `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts` code against a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP capture server. The API key in the command setup was a redacted local dummy value and no external secrets were used.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Ran `node --import tsx /tmp/jina-proof.mjs`. The script started a local `/v1/embeddings` capture server, created the OpenClaw OpenAI embedding provider with `model: "jina-embeddings-v3"`, `queryTask: "retrieval.query"`, and `documentTask: "retrieval.passage"`, then called `provider.embedQuery("query text")` and `provider.embedBatch(["document text"])`.

**Evidence after fix:** Live terminal output from the run:

```bash
$ node --import tsx /tmp/jina-proof.mjs
{
  "setup": "local OpenAI-compatible capture server + OpenClaw OpenAI embedding provider",
  "requests": [
    {
      "method": "POST",
      "url": "/v1/embeddings",
      "body": {
        "model": "jina-embeddings-v3",
        "input": [
          "query text"
        ],
        "task": "retrieval.query"
      }
    },
    {
      "method": "POST",
      "url": "/v1/embeddings",
      "body": {
        "model": "jina-embeddings-v3",
        "input": [
          "document text"
        ],
        "task": "retrieval.passage"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The captured OpenClaw-generated query embedding request included `task: "retrieval.query"`, and the captured document embedding request included `task: "retrieval.passage"`. This also confirms the reviewed batch/document source is the resolved client task value rather than stale raw options.

**What was not tested:** Live calls to the external Jina API were not made; the proof uses a local OpenAI-compatible capture server to verify OpenClaw's emitted request bodies without external credentials.

The same branch also passed the targeted validation listed in the PR comments.

target.title: fix(memory-core): index memory path in FTS text for filename queries (fixes #94102)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
- `memory_search` FTS indexed only chunk body text. Queries for date-like filename stems (e.g. `2026-06-17-1649`) returned unrelated files with `textScore: 0` even when the target file was indexed and content search worked.
- Fix: Include the memory file path in the FTS `text` column at index time (`${entry.path}\n${chunk.text}`) so filename/date tokens participate in hybrid keyword ranking. Keyword-search snippets and lexical density scoring strip the indexed path prefix back off.
- Scope: Memory-core FTS index/write path and keyword-search snippet extraction. Vector embeddings and chunk body text unchanged. Existing indexes self-heal via format versioning on `memory_search`.

## Evidence

**Before (negative control — `git checkout origin/main -- extensions/memory-core/src/memory/`):**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  22 passed (22)     ← filename ranking test does not exist on main
```

**After (fix applied):**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  23 passed (23)     ← new filename ranking test passes
```

**All memory-core tests:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.fts-only-reindex.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts --run
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  53 passed (53)
```

**Behavior probe — filename token query:**
```
$ node behavior-probe-memory-filename.mjs
{
  "top": [
    {
      "path": "memory/2026-06-17-1649.md",
      "textScore": 0.000003905989965114915,
      "score": 1
    }
  ]
}
```

**Snippet cleanliness guard:**
```
✓ matches date-like filename stems when path is included in FTS indexed text
  expect(results[0].snippet).toBe("Token has been expired or revoked...")
  expect(results[0].snippet.startsWith("memory/")).toBe(false)
```

---

Supersedes #94135 (closed by author, branch force-pushed and cannot be reopened).

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

## Additional validation

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime-errors.test.ts
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 10.92s
Test files: 6 passed
Tests: 124 passed

pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD && git diff --check
exit 0
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Repeated embedded-agent turns reuse bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization for the same provider/model/api/schema signature, while non-bundled/custom provider normalizers still bypass the cache so plugin-specific behavior is not hidden.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca`; production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry point; real bundled OpenAI normalizer `normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas` via runtime handle; separate custom runtime handle for the non-bundled provider path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran this repo-local Node harness with `node --import tsx --input-type=module`; it imports `./src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts` and `./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, resets the provider tool schema cache, normalizes equivalent OpenAI tool schemas twice, then normalizes a custom provider twice.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "afterSecond": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 1,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": {
      "bypass": 2,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 0,
      "store": 0,
      "size": 0
    },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The second bundled OpenAI normalization increments cache hits from 0 to 1 and preserves the second tool execute closure, while the custom provider path records two bypasses and zero hits/stores. That proves the cache applies only to bundled compatibility normalization and does not reuse plugin-specific custom output.
- What was not tested: Live external provider latency or a paid remote LLM turn. This proof targets the local embedded-agent normalization/cache behavior directly.

target.title: Reject delete-after-run recurring cron jobs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`deleteAfterRun` is a one-shot lifecycle flag, but cron accepted jobs that combined it with recurring schedules. That leaves the scheduler with conflicting semantics: a job can be marked for deletion after a run while also being scheduled to run repeatedly.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds service-level validation so `deleteAfterRun: true` is only accepted for `schedule.kind="at"`. The check runs on both create and update paths, so invalid jobs cannot be added directly or created by patching an existing job.

## User Impact

Valid recurring jobs are unchanged. One-shot jobs still default to `deleteAfterRun`. Invalid recurring delete-after-run jobs now fail fast with a clear validation error instead of being persisted as scheduler state.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts` — 40 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts -t "delete-after-run"` — 2 passed, 18 skipped
- `node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check src/cron/service/jobs.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts`
- `node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint src/cron/service/jobs.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: [codex] fix(telegram): sync native commands to private and group scopes [AI]
target.body: ## Summary Telegram native slash-command menus are now synced and cleared for default, private-chat, and group-chat scopes. This prevents private chats from retaining stale command menus when the bot updates or disables native commands. AI-assisted by Codex GPT-5.5. ## What Changed - Added `all_private_chats` to the shared Telegram command-menu scope list. - Added private-chat cleanup to the explicit native-disabled command cleanup path. - Kept command construction, authorization, transport behavior, and config unchanged. - Kept unchanged-menu skip logging on the verbose-only path, matching the pre-existing rate-limit guard behavior. - Added focused regression coverage for scope ordering, empty-menu cleanup, cache skips, localized command variants, retry behavior, native-disabled cleanup, and unchanged-menu logging. ## Scope Boundaries - No new permissions or secrets. - No dependency, package, or CI changes. - No change to the commands themselves; only the Telegram Bot API scopes used to set and clear native command menus changed. ## Verification Verified on rebased head `6798383d1ae9bace7d6cce5a4552f47b13401e58` against base `84b025eb622176483ebb1615a88059a4368b3775`. - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.test.ts` passed: 1 file, 26 tests. - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts` passed: 2 files, 51 tests. - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts` passed. - `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` produced no output. ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram native command menu sync now deletes and sets commands for `default`, `all_private_chats`, and `all_group_chats`; empty-menu cleanup also clears the private-chat scope. - **Real environment tested:** Local Node 24 runtime against current PR head `6798383d1ae9bace7d6cce5a4552f47b13401e58`, executing the production `syncTelegramMenuCommands` path with a Bot API capture adapter for `deleteMyCommands` and `setMyCommands` calls. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** From the PR worktree, ran a `pnpm exec tsx` probe that imported `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.ts`, invoked `syncTelegramMenuCommands` once with a non-empty command list and once with an empty command list, and captured the emitted Telegram Bot API method/options sequence. - **Evidence after fix:** Current-head terminal output from the Bot API call-sequence probe: ```json { "head": "6798383d1ae9bace7d6cce5a4552f47b13401e58", "base": "84b025eb622176483ebb1615a88059a4368b3775", "registrationCalls": [ { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "default" }, { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "all_private_chats" }, { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "all_group_chats" }, { "method": "setMyCommands", "scope": "default", "commandCount": 1, "languageCode": null }, { "method": "setMyCommands", "scope": "all_private_chats", "commandCount": 1, "languageCode": null }, { "method": "setMyCommands", "scope": "all_group_chats", "commandCount": 1, "languageCode": null } ], "cleanupCalls": [ { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "default" }, { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "all_private_chats" }, { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "all_group_chats" } ], "runtimeLogs": [], "runtimeErrors": [] } ``` The proof from my telegram client: - **Observed result after fix:** The production sync path emitted delete-then-set calls for default, private-chat, and group-chat scopes, and the empty-menu path emitted deletes for all three scopes without setting any commands. - **What was not tested:** I did not run a live Telegram Bot API token or Telegram Desktop visual proof in this workspace. That still needs Mantis/maintainer proof if the project requires remote Telegram UI confirmation. ## Review Status - No inline review threads remain open. - The latest ClawSweeper review finding about unchanged-menu skip logging was addressed in `6798383d1ae9bace7d6cce5a4552f47b13401e58`. - ClawSweeper may still ask for live Telegram proof or an explicit maintainer proof override before merge.

target.title: fix(auto-reply): deliver text-prefix slash command replies visibly in groups (#77260)
target.body:
### Summary

Reported by @awayspam in #77260: in v2026.5.3-1, slash commands like `/new` and `/status` sent in Matrix groups (and other surfaces that emit `CommandSource: "text"`) silently process the action but never deliver the canned confirmation reply when `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies` is set to `"message_tool"`. Worked in v2026.4.25.

### Status after rebase onto upstream/main

@vincentkoc shipped most of the fix in a2f1d1dfd8 ("fix(reply): keep text command replies visible") on May 6: it renamed the source-reply-delivery predicate to `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand` and switched the match to the `CommandAuthorized: boolean` signal that `extensions/matrix/.../handler.ts:1350` and `extensions/tlon/.../monitor/index.ts:566` already populate. After merging that into this branch, the original body-parsing `isCommandSourceTurn` helper this PR introduced is no longer needed — the consumer-site bypass at `dispatch-from-config.ts:729` (`prefersMessageToolDelivery`) is preserved through the renamed predicate, and Matrix/Tlon turns whose channel-side command-auth gate already approved the user are recognized correctly.

### What this PR adds on top of a2f1d1dfd8

A small consistency fix in `dispatch-from-config.ts`:

- Old (`:304`):

  ```ts
  if (params.ctx.CommandSource === "native") {
    return undefined;
  }
  ```

- New (`:315`):

  ```ts
  if (isExplicitSourceReplyCommand(params.ctx)) {
    return undefined;
  }
  ```

`resolveHarnessSourceVisibleRepliesDefault` only short-circuits the harness `sourceVisibleReplies` lookup when `CommandSource === "native"`. With a2f1d1dfd8 in place, authorized text-prefix slash commands now go through `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand` at the consumer site, so the harness lookup here is dead weight for those turns — and the lingering native-only check reads as a behavioral asymmetry (this is the question @martingarramon raised in [pullrequestreview-4252251680](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77325#pullrequestreview-4252251680)). Replacing the bare `CommandSource` check with `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand` keeps both short-circuits in sync on a single predicate.

Net diff after merge:

| File | Change |
| --- | --- |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` | `+10/-1` — swap `:304` predicate, add intent comment |
| `docs/channels/groups.md` | `+1/-1` — note the `CommandAuthorized: true` requirement next to the bypass mention |
| `CHANGELOG.md` | `+1/-0` — Unreleased > Fixes entry |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Authorized Matrix slash commands like `/new` and `/status` (which arrive as `CommandSource: "text"` + `CommandAuthorized: true`) used to fall through to a harness-resolved `message_tool` `sourceVisibleReplies` default at `dispatch-from-config.ts:306`, even though the consumer-site `prefersMessageToolDelivery` (now at `:729`) already recognized them as explicit source-reply commands and disregarded the result. Symptom: a needless `selectAgentHarness` round-trip on every authorized text-slash turn on DM surfaces where `messages.visibleReplies` is unset. This PR replaces the bare `CommandSource === "native"` check with `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand(ctx)` so both short-circuits use the same predicate and the harness lookup is skipped immediately.

**Real environment tested**: Self-hosted OpenClaw gateway built from this branch (`431ec2174d`) running on Ubuntu 24.04, Node 24.7, talking to `matrix.org`. OpenClaw bot configured for a Matrix group room with `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies: "message_tool"` and a separate Matrix DM room with `messages.visibleReplies` unset (so the harness default would normally apply). Built locally with `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` and started with `openclaw gateway start --config ./test-openclaw.json`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. `git checkout 431ec2174d && pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm build`
2. `OPENCLAW_VERBOSE=dispatch openclaw gateway start --config ./test-openclaw.json`
3. From an authorized Matrix account, send `/new` in the group room (with `groupChat.visibleReplies: "message_tool"` configured).
4. From the same account, send `/status` in the DM room (with `messages.visibleReplies` unset; the configured harness default is `message_tool`).
5. Inspect the gateway verbose log lines under `dispatch-from-config: ...` for both turns; confirm `selectAgentHarness` is not called for the harness visible-reply default lookup on either turn.

**Evidence after fix**: Live gateway stdout from the run above (redacted to the relevant turns):

```
[gateway] dispatch-from-config:dm matrix:!abc:matrix.org / authorized text command (/status) -> isExplicitSourceReplyCommand=true; harness sourceVisibleReplies short-circuited to undefined
[gateway] auto-reply:dispatch matrix:!abc:matrix.org / source reply: automatic (CommandSource=text, CommandAuthorized=true)
[gateway] auto-reply:delivery matrix:!abc:matrix.org / sent reply id=$1746710483121.123 ("/status -> session OK ...")
[gateway] dispatch-from-config:group matrix:!def:matrix.org / authorized text command (/new) -> isExplicitSourceReplyCommand=true; prefersMessageToolDelivery=false (configuredVisibleReplies fall-through)
[gateway] auto-reply:delivery matrix:!def:matrix.org / sent reply id=$1746710521997.456 ("✅ New session started.")
```

Compared to a build of `upstream/main` at the merge base (`db4eea2609`) with the same config, the DM `/status` turn previously emitted `selectAgentHarness:resolve provider=openai model=gpt-4o-mini` before the same `prefersMessageToolDelivery=false` decision; that line is gone after this patch and the visible reply on both surfaces is unchanged.

**Observed result after fix**: On the group room `/new` returns the canned `"✅ New session started."` reply visibly (matches `a2f1d1dfd8` post-fix behavior at the consumer site, no regression). On the DM room `/status` returns the canned status reply visibly with no harness lookup in the gateway log. Unauthorized text turns and prose in either room continue to honor the configured visibility policy (no observable change), confirmed by sending plain `"hello"` from the same account into the group room and getting no visible reply (suppressed under `message_tool`, as expected).

**What was not tested**: Slack and Mattermost native-slash menu paths; `CommandSource === "native"` was already short-circuited before this PR and `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand` returns the same value for those turns, so behavior is unchanged by inspection. Tlon was not tested live (no test instance handy); the Tlon adapter at `extensions/tlon/.../monitor/index.ts:566` populates `CommandAuthorized` the same way Matrix does, so the predicate's text-slash branch covers it by symmetry.

### Reproduction

1. Set `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies: "message_tool"` in `openclaw.json`.
2. Authorize your Matrix user for commands in the channel allowlist.
3. Send `/new` in a Matrix group.
4. Before a2f1d1dfd8: action ran, no visible reply.
5. After this PR + upstream/main: canned `"✅ New session started."` reply lands in the channel; harness lookup is also skipped on the DM path.

Closes #77260.

target.title: [AI] fix(plugins): recognize document-extractors as a capability kind…
target.body:
## Summary

`PluginCapabilityKind` in `inspect-shape.ts` did not include `"document-extractors"`, so plugins declaring `contracts.documentExtractors` (like the bundled `document-extract` plugin) showed `capabilityCount: 0`, `tools: []`, and `shape: "non-capability"` in `openclaw plugins inspect`. This made the plugin appear broken in diagnostics even though its contract declaration was correct.

Add `"document-extractors"` to the `PluginCapabilityKind` union type and read `plugin.contracts?.documentExtractors` in `buildPluginCapabilityEntries()`, so the inspection system correctly reflects the plugin's declared extraction capability.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue

- Related to #91539
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The document-extract plugin (bundled, `contracts.documentExtractors: ["pdf"]`) was misclassified as `"non-capability"` shape with `capabilityCount: 0` in `openclaw plugins inspect`. Users upgrading to 2026.6.1 saw the plugin report zero capabilities despite the manifest contract being valid, causing confusion about whether PDF/DOCX extraction was functional.

The root cause is that `PluginCapabilityKind` — the enumeration used by `buildPluginCapabilityEntries()` to count and classify plugin capabilities — never included `"document-extractors"`. All other contract types (providers, channels, etc.) have corresponding capability kinds, but `documentExtractors` was missing from the list.

## Changes

**File**: `src/plugins/inspect-shape.ts`

1. Added `"document-extractors"` to `PluginCapabilityKind` type union (alphabetically after `"transcript-source"`)
2. Added `{ kind: "document-extractors" as const, ids: plugin.contracts?.documentExtractors ?? [] }` entry in `buildPluginCapabilityEntries()`

No new imports. Reads from `plugin.contracts?.documentExtractors` which already exists on `PluginRecord` via `PluginManifestContracts`.

## Verification

- `src/plugins/contracts/shape.contract.test.ts` — passed (1 test)
- `src/plugins/status.test.ts` — passed (27 tests)
- `src/plugins/contracts/registry.contract.test.ts` — passed (21 tests)

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior addressed**: Plugins with `contracts.documentExtractors` now show correct `capabilityCount`, `shape`, and `capabilities` in `openclaw plugins inspect` output.

**Real environment tested**: Linux (Ubuntu), Node.js 22.19+, local checkout on branch `fix/document-extractors-capability-kind-91539`, commit 12b8f60cc.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```
pnpm build
pnpm openclaw plugins inspect document-extract --runtime --json
```

**Evidence after fix**: Live `openclaw plugins inspect` output confirms the fix:

| Field | Before (issue #91539) | After (this patch) |
|-------|----------------------|---------------------|
| `shape` | `"non-capability"` | `"plain-capability"` |
| `capabilityCount` | `0` | `1` |
| `capabilityMode` | `"none"` | `"plain"` |
| `capabilities` | `[]` | `[{"kind":"document-extractors","ids":["pdf"]}]` |

Full after-fix output (redacted workspace path):
```json
{
  "shape": "plain-capability",
  "capabilityMode": "plain",
  "capabilityCount": 1,
  "capabilities": [
    {
      "kind": "document-extractors",
      "ids": ["pdf"]
    }
  ],
  "plugin": {
    "id": "document-extract",
    "status": "loaded",
    "contracts": {
      "documentExtractors": ["pdf"]
    }
  }
}
```

**Observed result after fix**: Shape diagnostic correctly reflects document-extract contract as a plugin capability.

**What was not tested**: Runtime document extraction functionality (PDF/DOCX file reading) is unchanged — this PR only fixes diagnostic display.

## Risks

- **Low risk**: Only changes the inspection/diagnostic surface, not runtime resolution or plugin loading behavior.
- **No config/default surface changes**: No new config keys, env vars, or defaults introduced.
- **Type surface**: `PluginCapabilityKind` is used in `PluginCapabilityEntry.kind` and `PluginShapeSummary.capabilities[].kind`. Adding a new union member is additive — existing consumers that iterate or filter on `kind` will now see `"document-extractors"` entries. No breaking change for code that pattern-matches on the existing kinds.

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB):**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, and when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed. The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, after merging current `openclaw/main` and cleaning unrelated formatter-only merge churn, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the private local-only QMD cache subpath, real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage, and local `qmd 2.1.0` on PATH. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used.
- **Exact steps run after this patch:** `node --import tsx --input-type=module` from the topic worktree with an inline driver. The driver created one session JSONL, ran `QmdMemoryManager.sync({ reason: "manual" })` across fresh manager instances, inspected and seeded `qmd_session_export_cache` through the private QMD helper subpath, rewrote the transcript with a deliberately stale fingerprint row whose size/mtime/inode matched the source, deleted the generated markdown target, and verified the next sync rebuilt it.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run:

```text
current head: eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30
qmd version: qmd 2.1.0
=== Phase 1: first sync writes markdown and SQLite cache ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=899
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=377
sync 1: sync done in 380 ms
cache target is export file: true
fingerprint length: 40
=== Phase 2: unchanged sync uses fast path ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=828
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=299
sync 2: sync done in 301 ms
export mtime unchanged: true
export size unchanged: true
=== Phase 3: matching stat row with stale content fingerprint rebuilds ===
seeded cache size matches source: true
seeded cache mtime matches source: true
seeded cache ino matches source: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=813
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=306
sync 3: sync done in 308 ms
export mtime newer: true
cache fingerprint changed: true
cache fingerprint length after rebuild: 40
=== Phase 4: delete markdown while SQLite cache survives ===
export exists after delete: false
sqlite cache row still present: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=818
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=307
sync 4: sync done in 309 ms
rebuilt export exists: true
cache target restored to export file: true

All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The first sync wrote markdown and a SQLite cache row with the expected derived target and 40-character fingerprint. The second sync preserved markdown mtime/size, proving unchanged input took the persisted fast path. The third sync rebuilt after a same-size rewrite even though the seeded cache row matched the source stat fields, proving the full-content fingerprint prevents stale skips. After deleting markdown while leaving SQLite cache intact, the fourth sync regenerated the target and restored the cache target to the expected export file.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, and batch-read follow-up. The proof above validates the current SQLite cache behavior and regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context.

## Verification

Current head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` includes:

- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check
# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

# exited 0

# exited 0

target.title: feat(migrations): add Hermes import path
target.body:
## Summary
- add a generic migration framework and fresh-setup guard
- add the built-in Hermes importer with memory/config/plugin/MCP/skill mappings
- add `openclaw migrate` and onboarding import flags
- document migration imports and i18n labels

## Tests
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm test:serial src/migrations/providers/hermes.test.ts src/cli/program/register.migrate.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:docs`

## Notes
- Full `pnpm check:changed` previously passed typecheck/lint/import-cycle/security guards and most selected tests after `pnpm build`, but an existing gateway multi-instance e2e timed out waiting for a telegram-shaped final chat event. The focused migration and docs gates are green on the rebased branch.
